feed.title: SAP Community - ABAP Cloud
feed.subtitle: ABAP Cloud blog posts in SAP Community

entry.title: Week 1 : Set up the Basic Trial account enabled with Joule for Developers
entry.content:
The Basic Trial offering for SAP Build has been extended with a new workbook called 'Fusion Development with ABAP Cloud in SAP Build'. You can explore this workbook for the Basic Trial offering around SAP Build, that shows how developers can now create new ABAP Cloud projects, or work on existing ones, from the SAP Build lobby and then navigate to their ABAP development environment – the ABAP Development Tools for Eclipse (ADT).

Task 1 : Set up the Basic Trial account and add the ABAP instance in ADT.

Click the following URL:   

https://www.sap.com/products/technology-platform/build/trial.html

In the following screen click on "Try Now". 

You will receive a user with a password that will allow you to authenticate at the landing page of the basic trial in the respective region for your user, e.g. https://trials.cfapps.eu10-004.hana.ondemand.com/ .  

Basic Trial Registration

By using the credentials presented in the web page you will get access to some workbooks that cover different scenarios. 

The ABAP related workbook is named Fusion Development with ABAP Cloud in SAP Build.

Select the highlighted workbook.

Choose the Highlighted Workbook

Click ‘Start Workbook’

Start WorkBook

Note : It may take up to 30 minutes to fully activate your user account in the SAP BTP ABAP Environment. 

Read the details mentioned in Overview of the Section ‘Set up your development environment’. You can skip Step 1 and 2 if you already have ADT installed but make sure you are on the latest version of ADT for all the features to work fine. Once the installation is done directly move to Step 3 - 'Create ABAP Cloud Project in SAP Build' only until adding the basic trial instance to ADT. We will be it using the URL marked in the below screenshot.

Note:- Once you have the system added in ADT you can close the workbook.

System URL

Task 2 :  Create a package in the new ABAP Cloud Project 
Once you see the new ABAP Cloud Project (TDI ) added in ADT, create a package with the naming convention ZAC_<your community ID>.

Note: Please make sure you are using the latest version of ADT, otherwise package creation will not work.

To get the community ID launch the URL : https://community.sap.com/ and click on the person icon as shown in below screenshot and go to ‘My Content’.

Community Page

Once the below page opens up please copy the number after user-id in the URL to get your community ID.

Community ID

Note:- We will be validating your activities by checking the folder against your community ID. So make sure all your development objects are saved inside this package.

Task 3 :  Learn about the SAP Joule for Developers, ABAP AI Capabilities

To prepare for the challenge familiarise yourself with the scope first by going through the following help document.

https://help.sap.com/docs/abap-cloud/abap-development-tools-user-guide/joule-for-developers-abap-ai-capabilities-f14ebffef77b41bfb0746c33dcb70e84?locale=en-US

Now you are ready to take up the challenge. See you next week !!

entry.title: ABAP Developer Challenge - Joule for Developers using Basic Trial
entry.content:
Let’s kick off this year’s Developer Challenge with a topic close to home: ABAP.

With AI dominating conversations across the tech world, we thought it would be exciting for our community to explore Joule for Developers this month.

SAP Joule for Developers brings embedded AI capabilities to SAP Build and ABAP, delivered through Joule. It helps accelerate the development of apps and extensions, reduce development costs, and enhance overall developer productivity and proficiency.

SAP Joule for Developers is engineered for ABAP excellence by

  • Leveraging SAP’s ABAP large language model trained on enterprise-grade code.
  • Generating data models, app logic, and unit tests with precision.
  • Migrating custom ABAP code faster with documentation chat, code explanation, and issue diagnostics.

The Challenge:

  For this challenge we will be using Basic Trial offering for SAP Build that includes a SAP BTP ABAP Environment with Joule for Developers. During the first week we will focus on setting up the ABAP instance from the Basic Trial in ADT and then will explore some of the key functionalities of Joule for Developers.

How to Participate:

To participate in and complete this challenge, simply follow these steps:

  • Complete the challenge assigned for each week.
  • Create a package with your community ID in the format mentioned in the Week1 challenge.
  • Post your package name in the thread for each week.
  • We will review your work inside the package with your community ID.

Challenge Timeline

The challenge will span for 4 weeks starting on Monday March 2nd 2026 and ending on March 23rd 2026. We will keep the challenge open till the end of the month to accomodate anyone who is running late 🙂. To count towards the overall monthly challenge, you must complete and submit each week's task. Make sure to revisit this blog post to discover the upcoming week's challenge!

March 2nd, 2026   – Start of Challenge: Week 1

March 9th, 2026    –  Week 2 

March 16th, 2026  – Week 3 :

March 23rd, 2026  – Week 4 :

So let’s get started.

Rewards

Upon submitting all required entries by the end of the challenge, you'll earn an SAP Community badge for participating in the SAP Developer Challenge for March. Please ensure to follow all the instructions mentioned in this blogpost or the respective challenge discussions.

We are excited to hear your experience about the challenge and the learning adventure.

Best of Luck !!!

entry.title: A New System-Based ABAP Certification Is Here - What Developers Need to Know
entry.content:
After a long wait, it’s finally here: a new practical ABAP certification exam designed to test what really matters. This time, you’ll be working hands-on in a live SAP system.

🚀SAP Certified - Backend Developer - ABAP Cloud (Beta Version)

 If you’re planning to take it, here’s what you should know - and how to prepare effectively.

💡Good to know!

The new exam https://learning.sap.com/certifications/sap-certified-associate-back-end-developer-abap-cloud focuses on solving practical development tasks based on the content of:

  • Basic ABAP Programming (S4D400)
  • Intermediate ABAP Programming (S4D401)
  • Data Modelling in ABAP Dictionary and ABAP Core Data Services (S4D430)

You’ll work in an ABAP environment on SAP BTP, using the ABAP language version for cloud development.

If your background is primarily on-premise ABAP, this is a mindset shift.

 ℹ️We’re currently in the Beta phase of the certification exam.

What that means for you:

  •  3-hour exam instead of the planned 4-hour version
  • Same full certification as the final release - no “beta” label
  • Early opportunity before the scope expands

 

After Beta, the exam will be extended to 4 hours with broader coverage.

Don’t work harder, work smarter. Secure your spot now!

 

The Environment – ABAP Cloud

ABAP Cloud promotes:

  • Model-driven development
  • Use of Core Data Services (CDS)
  • ABAP RESTful Application Programming Model (RAP)
  • Automatic exposure of services (OData/Web APIs)
    All leading to faster, more scalable apps and services. 

🤓Hint: Before you start, be sure to update your Eclipse and ADT installations to the latest versions.

 

Why Practice Systems Are Critical

The certification system will resemble the training and practice systems available in learning environments - but it won’t be identical.

 The practice systems, including the exercise handbooks and system setup guides to practice and prepare, can be found here:

  • Basic ABAP Programming S4D400
  • Intermediate ABAP Programming S4D401
  • Data Modelling in ABAP Dictionary and ABAP Core Data Services S4D430

🤓Hint: Please refer to the course file that is downloaded when clicking the access button for further log on information

 

 Preparation is Essential.

 The practice systems give you access to:

  • The same underlying data model used in the exam
  • Identical table and field names
  • A comparable development setup

 Getting familiar with the structure of the data model and working with ABAP Development Tools for Eclipse (ADT) will save you valuable time during the exam.

 ⏲ And time matters!

You’ll have three hours to complete the exam.  Three hours go faster than you think!

🤓Hint: Joule for Developers is available within the exam system. We recommend using it while working through the tasks to help you complete them more efficiently.

 

 How to Prepare

Along with the practice Systems mentioned above, the Learning Journey  Acquiring Core ABAP Skills will help you to prepare for your exam.

 

Good Luck 🍀 and Happy Learning!

 

For more information on the Reimagine Certification initiative, please read: Reimagining Certification for the Way We Work Toda... - SAP Community

 

SAP Learning Services Topic Page 
Ask questions about Training and follow 
Read other Training blog posts and follow

entry.title: Data Integration in ABAP Cloud: Data Exposure using SQL services
entry.content:
Introduction

Data integration addresses the data exchange between two or more communication partners without a relation to a business process.

Using the SQL service binding and the open database connectivity (ODBC), you can implement data integration scenarios where data in the ABAP system can be accessed from an external system or application outside the ABAP system.

Data integration in ABAP Cloud mainly focuses on SQL-based integration between systems. There are two main approaches:

  • Data federation: live access from an ABAP system to an external database (for example, SAP HANA Cloud).
  • Data replication: copying data from one system to another, usually delta-based, so only changes are transferred.

From an ABAP system perspective, data integration has two dimensions:

  • Data exposure: defining which data is exposed and how external systems can access it.
  • Data consumption: accessing and using data that resides outside the ABAP system.

This post focuses on data exposure using SQL services.

Data Exposure from ABAP Cloud System using SQL Services

In ABAP systems, database objects are managed by the ABAP layer, meaning, by data dictionary, application server and so on. Direct database access is therefore discouraged and often impossible in cloud environments. Does this mean SQL access is not possible? Not at all!

With SQL services, ABAP opens a secure SQL port at the application server layer. External applications can query exposed ABAP objects using SQL, without direct database access.

The key benefits are as follows:

  • Data exposure is based on CDS views and the ABAP Cloud service model.
  • Authorization is handled via ABAP users and roles, not database users.
  • Business data restrictions (for example, region-based access) are fully supported.
  • Requests benefit from application server buffering.
  • Lifecycle management is fully integrated into the ABAP development process.

As a developer in the ABAP environment, you can expose CDS view entities in an ABAP system for external consumption using SQL services. An SQL service is a standardized protocol of the Application Server ABAP providing SQL-level access to ABAP-managed database objects, such as CDS view entities, for consumers outside the system. The SQL service can be specified as a type of service binding.

SAP also provides an ABAP ODBC driver, which can be used by any ODBC-compliant tool (such as Microsoft Excel). From newer releases, this driver is included in SAP HANA Cloud and can be used to create remote connections to ABAP systems.

Compared to the OData interface, the SQL service exposure has the advantage that it allows unrestricted SQL access to all exposed ABAP CDS view entities. Data from different entities can be joined in an ad-hoc fashion and data can be aggregated for SQL queries.

Data Exposure-Technical User Scenario

This post covered the technical user (privileged) scenario, which is ideal for system-to-system integration. In this scenario, the technical user has unrestricted access to all exposed data.

Privileged vs. Business User Access

  • Privileged access uses a technical communication user with full access to exposed data.
  • Business user access applies authorization checks and business data restrictions based on user roles.

Data integration can be considered from the point of view of a single ABAP system, the so called “In inbound data integration scenarios” in which you define and set up inbound services in the ABAP system, so that you can expose data from the ABAP system to external systems and tools.

To start with, in your SAP BTP ABAP system, create a new ABAP Cloud project and add a package, for example “ZTRAVEL_000”. Then do the following steps:

 Design-Time Setup

We create an example with travel scenario.

  1. Create a database table.
EndUserText.label : 'Travel Data'
@AbapCatalog.enhancement.category : #NOT_EXTENSIBLE
@AbapCatalog.tableCategory : #TRANSPARENT
@AbapCatalog.deliveryClass : #A
@AbapCatalog.dataMaintenance : #RESTRICTED
define table ztravel_000 {

  key client            : abap.clnt not null;
  key travel_id         : abap.numc(8) not null;
  agency_id             : abap.numc(6);
  customer_id           : abap.numc(6);
  begin_date            : abap.dats;
  end_date              : abap.dats;
  @Semantics.amount.currencyCode : 'ztravel_000.currency_code'
  booking_fee           : abap.curr(16,2);
  @Semantics.amount.currencyCode : 'ztravel_000.currency_code'
  total_price           : abap.curr(16,2);
  currency_code         : abap.cuky;
  description           : abap.sstring(1024);
  overall_status        : abap.char(1);
  created_by            : abp_creation_user;
  created_at            : abp_creation_tstmpl;
  last_changed_by       : abp_locinst_lastchange_user;
  last_changed_at       : abp_locinst_lastchange_tstmpl;
  local_last_changed_at : abp_lastchange_tstmpl;

}

Save and activate it.

2. Create a CDS view on top of the table.

AbapCatalog.viewEnhancementCategory: [#NONE]
@AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #NOT_REQUIRED
@EndUserText.label: 'Core Data Service Entity for travel'
@Metadata.ignorePropagatedAnnotations: true

define root view entity ZR_TRAVEL_000 as select from ZTRAVEL_000
{
  key travel_id as TravelID,
  agency_id as AgencyID,
  customer_id as CustomerID,
  begin_date as BeginDate,
  end_date as EndDate,
  @Semantics.amount.currencyCode: 'CurrencyCode'
  booking_fee as BookingFee,
  @Semantics.amount.currencyCode: 'CurrencyCode'
  total_price as TotalPrice,
  @Consumption.valueHelpDefinition: [ {
    entity.name: 'I_CurrencyStdVH', 
    entity.element: 'Currency', 
    useForValidation: true
  } ]
  currency_code as CurrencyCode,
  description as Description,
  overall_status as OverallStatus,
  @Semantics.user.createdBy: true
  created_by as CreatedBy,
  @Semantics.systemDateTime.createdAt: true
  created_at as CreatedAt,
  @Semantics.user.localInstanceLastChangedBy: true
  last_changed_by as LastChangedBy,
  @Semantics.systemDateTime.localInstanceLastChangedAt: true
  last_changed_at as LastChangedAt,
  @Semantics.systemDateTime.lastChangedAt: true
  local_last_changed_at as LocalLastChangedAt
}
​

Save and activate it.

3. Create a class to populate the table and verify the data.

CLASS zcl_travel_data_gen_000 DEFINITION
 PUBLIC
  FINAL
  CREATE PUBLIC .

  PUBLIC SECTION.

    INTERFACES if_oo_adt_classrun.
  PROTECTED SECTION.
  PRIVATE SECTION.
ENDCLASS.

CLASS zcl_travel_data_gen_000 IMPLEMENTATION.

  METHOD if_oo_adt_classrun~main.
    DATA:
      group_id   TYPE string VALUE '000',
      attachment TYPE /dmo/attachment,
      file_name  TYPE /dmo/filename,
      mime_type  TYPE /dmo/mime_type.

*   clear data
    DELETE FROM ztravel_000.

    "insert travel demo data
    INSERT ztravel_000  FROM (
        SELECT
          FROM /dmo/travel AS travel
          FIELDS
            travel~travel_id        AS travel_id,
            travel~agency_id        AS agency_id,
            travel~customer_id      AS customer_id,
            travel~begin_date       AS begin_date,
            travel~end_date         AS end_date,
            travel~booking_fee      AS booking_fee,
            travel~total_price      AS total_price,
            travel~currency_code    AS currency_code,
            travel~description      AS description,
            CASE travel~status    "[N(New) | P(Planned) | B(Booked) | X(Cancelled)]
              WHEN 'N' THEN 'O'
              WHEN 'P' THEN 'O'
              WHEN 'B' THEN 'A'
              ELSE 'X'
            END                     AS overall_status,travel~createdby        AS created_by,
            travel~createdat        AS created_at,
            travel~lastchangedby    AS last_changed_by,
            travel~lastchangedat    AS last_changed_at,
            travel~lastchangedat    AS local_last_changed_at
            ORDER BY travel_id UP TO 10 ROWS
      ).
    COMMIT WORK.
    out->write( |Demo data generated for table ztravel_{ group_id }. | ).
  ENDMETHOD.
ENDCLASS.

 Save and activate it. Press F9 to run the class. On the table as well as the CDS view, press F8 to see the data.

4. Define a service definition exposing the CDS view.

@EndUserText.label: 'Service definition for service type SQL'
define service ZSQL_TRAVEL_000 {
  expose ZR_TRAVEL_000 as Travel;

5. Create a service binding of type SQL

Save and activate it.

6. Create a communication scenario and add the inbound object S_PRIVILEGED_SQL1.

In Authorization tab, Add authorization object S_SQL_View

Fill up the fields with the following’s values

S_SQL_VIEW is the SAP authorization object that controls which database views can be accessed via Open SQL. The field SQL_SCHEMA in that object is used to restrict the database schema of the SQL view that a user is allowed to access. So, the value for SQL_SCHEMA is your service binding  ZSQL_TRAVEL_000 .

Save and Publish the Scenario.

Configuration-Time Setup

In the Fiori launchpad of your development system:

1. Create a Communication Arrangement by creating a communication system (inbound only) and a communication user based on the Communication Scenario you published in design time.

2. In communication system section, click on “New” to create an “Inbound Only” system and an Inbound user. Keep the username and password for later usage in HANA Cloud DB. The inbound service will be added automatically. Click “Save” to create the communication arrangement.

Accessing ABAP Data from SAP HANA Cloud

First, you need to add the SAP HANA Cloud service in your SAP BTP account if you don’t have access to it.  In your account and through entitlement, assign the plan “tools” as described in SAP Help Portal:

Subscribing to the SAP HANA Cloud Administration Tools | SAP Help Portal .
In the service marketplace of your BTP account, choose the service, and create the instance

Through this instance you can navigate to your SAP HANA Cloud. Click on your instance to open HANA Cloud Central.  In “Instances” section, create an instance considering your requirements. In section” General”, give an Instance name and choose an Administrator password for your ADMIN user and keep it, this username and password is needed to connect to the HANA Cloud DB later. Then create your instance.

 In the “connections” section of your instance you will find all the information you need to connect to the database later.

 

Connect to SAP HANA Cloud using Visual Studio Code

Open visual studio code, install “HANA DB explorer” and use it to connect to your database.

Fill the required field to set the connection. Give the username and password you saved while creating the HANA Cloud DB instance.

Then create a remote source with ABAP ODBC adapter with the communication user and its password you have created as you create the communication system in the configuration time.

SQL Service HANA Cloud Remote Source Configuration

Use this code lines to set up the remote source:

CREATE PSE  <pse_name>;
CREATE CERTIFICATE <certificate_name> FROM '
-----BEGIN CERTIFICATE-----
.
. “Add the system certificate here”
.
-----END CERTIFICATE-----
';
ALTER PSE <pse_name> ADD CERTIFICATE <certificate_name>;

CREATE REMOTE SOURCE <remote_source_name> WITH CREDENTIAL TYPE 'PASSWORD' USING 'user=<inbound_user>;password=<inbound_user_password>;
SET PSE <pse_name> PURPOSE REMOTE SOURCE FOR REMOTE SOURCE <remote_source_name>;

Now we create a virtual table pointing to the exposed CDS entity:

CREATE VIRTUAL TABLE  <table_name> AT  <remote_source_name>."NULL"."ZSQL_TRAVEL_000"."Travel";

The virtual table acts as a live container for ABAP data. You can now run standard SQL queries against it and retrieve data directly from the ABAP system:

Privileged access enables communication between an SQL-based client and the ABAP system using a communication user and retrieve data.

In the next post, Data Integration in ABAP Cloud: Data Consumption using External Entities , we will discuss the second aspect - the data consumption - to consume data that is located somewhere else in our ABAP Cloud system.

entry.title: SAP’s Most Popular Certification Gets a Major Upgrade
entry.content:
March is officially the month of ABAP. SAP’s most popular certification for back-end developers, the Back-End Developer ABAP Cloud exam, is getting a major upgrade. At the same time, the SAP Community developer challenge for ABAP is going live, making this the perfect moment to sharpen your skills and validate your expertise.

 

A Reimagined Certification Experience

The ABAP Cloud certification exam has been redesigned. Moving beyond traditional question-based formats, the new exam introduces a performance-based, open-book experience. Learners now work directly in authentic SAP systems, navigating AI-supported, real-world scenarios that reflect actual project work. The focus is no longer on memorization; it’s about demonstrating practical skills that matter on the job.

 

What Does It Mean for You?

If you’ve been preparing for certification through the SAP Learning site, you’re already familiar with hands-on exercises and scenario-based tasks embedded in the learning journeys. The new exam format simply extends that approach. From the moment you begin your certification, the experience feels familiar because it mirrors how you’ve been learning all along.

Every practical exam, whether system-based or scenario-driven reflects the reality of SAP projects:

  • Timeboxed tasks performed in live SAP systems
  • Realistic customer and project scenarios
  • AI-supported simulations
  • Open-book access to resources such as SAP Help Portal, SAP Learning, SAP Community, and SAP Joule for Consultants (where applicable)
  • A clear emphasis on proving your ability to do the work

This shift represents something bigger: trust. Trust in learners who invest in building real skills and trust from employers who rely on SAP-certified professionals to deliver value from day one.

 

How to Get Certified as Backend Developer for ABAP Cloud

Earning your certification is a structured journey. Whether you are new to ABAP or looking to formalize years of experience, the following resources will help you build a strong foundation and deepen your expertise.

 

Recommended Courses to Build and Deepen Your Skills

Start with the fundamentals and progressively expand your knowledge:

Course: Learning Basic ABAP Programming

Course: Deepening Your ABAP Programming Knowledge

Course: Building Data Models with the ABAP Dictionary and ABAP Core Data Services

Course: Practicing Clean Core Extensibility for SAP S/4HANA Cloud

SAP Certification: Backend Developer - ABAP Cloud

 

Together, these courses prepare you not just to pass the exam but to succeed in real-world ABAP Cloud development. Learn more about the exam details in this community blog by @JenniferHauck.

 

 

Connect Live with SAP Experts and Strengthen Your Exam Readiness

Learning doesn’t have to be a solo experience. If you’re looking for direct interaction, practical tips, and expert guidance, join the upcoming live sessions available through your SAP Learning Hub subscription:

  • Getting Started with ABAP Cloud Development
  • Get Certified: SAP Certified – Back-End Developer ABAP Cloud
  • Stay Certified: Back-End Developer – ABAP Cloud

These sessions offer the opportunity to clarify questions, reinforce key concepts, and gain confidence before taking the exam.

 

Experience Hands-On Learning at SAP CodeJam Events Near You

If you prefer immersive, in-person learning, don’t miss the upcoming SAP CodeJam events. These workshops are designed to provide practical, guided experience with real SAP technologies in a collaborative environment.

Upcoming sessions include:

  • April 7: ABAP AI: Capabilities in SAP Joule for Developers CodeJam (São Paulo, Brazil)
  • April 8: ABAP AI: Capabilities in SAP Joule for Developers CodeJam (Londrina, Brazil)
  • April 23: ABAP Cloud and the RESTful Application Programming Model (Warsaw, Poland)

These events are an excellent opportunity to deepen your understanding, expand your network, and gain hands-on practice. Be sure to explore the full event calendar regularly, as new sessions are added on an ongoing basis.

 

Continue Your Learning with On-Demand Developer Content

Can’t attend live? Explore past Devtoberfest sessions, Tech Bytes, SAP CodeTalks, and more on the official SAP Developers YouTube channel. On-demand content allows you to learn at your own pace while staying connected to the latest innovations in ABAP and SAP development.

March is the perfect time to invest in your ABAP Cloud journey. With a certification experience designed to reflect real project work, you’re not just passing an exam, you’re demonstrating that you’re ready to build, extend, and innovate in the SAP ecosystem.

entry.title: ABAP Cloud Learning Map 2026
entry.content:
Pessoal,

SAP Liberou o Mapa de aprendizagem do ABAP Cloud,  ele oferece um bom ponto de partida para começar a conhecer as tecnologias, ferramentas, abordagens e características do ABAP Cloud, além de obter informações novas ou que estejam faltando, com base no seu próprio nível de conhecimento

Segue o link :

https://dam.sap.com/mac/app/p/pdf/asset/preview/7YM5vMP?ltr=a&rc=10&doi=SAP1280868

Muito interessante!

Aproveitem!

SAP S/4HANA Cloud ABAP Environment

  • Connect with developers worldwide

Being part of the community ensures you stay current and continue growing long after certification.

On-demand content allows you to learn at your own pace while staying connected to the latest innovations in ABAP and SAP development.

March is the perfect time to invest in your ABAP Cloud journey. With a certification experience designed to reflect real project work, you’re not just passing an exam, you’re demonstrating that you’re ready to build, extend, and innovate in the SAP ecosystem.

entry.title: Week 2 : Use Joule Chat to create ABAP RAP Application and explore the Explain feature
entry.content:
Last week we had completed the system set up and also created a package with your community ID. We had also gone through the help document on SAP Joule for Developers, ABAP AI Capabilities.

Now let us dive into the world of ABAP AI by creating an application where Joule will guide you during each step.

Quick Links

Please use this separate thread to ask your questions and discuss issues. 

Task 1: Create a ABAP RAP application using 'OData UI Service from Scratch’ wizard

It can be any application of your choice preferably non enterprise related to keep the challenge simple and more interesting. One field should be an amount field and another one a date field so that we can leverage J4D(Joule for Developers) to add some logic in the subsequent weeks. You should have 3 more fields which will be used to try out some of the J4D features.

Note: It is recommended to create all the fields while creating the app itself to avoid any manual coding later.

Example:- You can create a book store application consisting of fields ID, Title, Author, Price, Published Date, Stock, Discounted Price, BookAge. Here the last 3 fields can be used to try out the Joule for Developers (J4D) features. You are free to choose any dataset and logic combinations.

  1. Right click on the package ZAC_<your community ID> created and select ‘Generate ABAP Repository Objects’Generate ABAP Repository Objects’
  2. In the below popup that appears choose ‘Odata UI Service from Scratch’ and click on ‘Next’ two times.Odata UI Service from Scratch
  3. Click ‘Next’ again.Click Next
  4. In the next screen make sure you get the Joule Help to the right of Wizard popup. Here in the section ‘Ask Joule to update the fields’ can provide your prompts to create the application you like by specifying the fields and other details. Hit enter. Note: It is better to create all objects ending with your <community ID> to avoid any naming conflicts. Try to specify this in the prompt.Joule option in WizardNote: Make sure to select the option -  ‘Transactional with Draft’ highlighted in the above screenshot is selected to enable ‘Create’ option in the application getting created. For single entity sometimes it is set as ‘Without Draft’.
  5. Joule will now propose the Entity and Fields based on your prompt. Please note that we still should verify whether the suggestions are accurate for our requirements. Here you can notice that sometimes Joule suggests to change the Service Configuration to ‘Transactional Without Draft’, we can handle this in next step.Joule Field Proposal
  6. Once you are satisfied click on ‘Accept’ and you can see that the ‘Artifact Suffix’ if specified and the ‘Project Name’ gets added as shown in the below screenshot.           Here remember to change the Service Configuration to ‘Transactional with Draft’ and click ‘Next’.Joule Accept Proposal
  7. Joule lists the artifacts that will be created. Verify them again and click on ‘Next’ and then ‘Finish’ on the next screen. 
  8. Refresh the package and check all the artifacts have been created. Publish the Service Binding and preview the application.
  9. The application will open and should have a ‘Create’ button. Here you can either create a data generator class and add some simple logic to fill data / Use the ‘Create’ option in the app to create some sample records. ( Please create 4-5 entries in any case ). You can also adjust the metadata if you like.Application Created

For more information: https://help.sap.com/docs/abap-cloud/abap-development-tools-user-guide/use-odata-ui-service-from-scratch-wizard?locale=en-US

Task 2: Add a simple logic in the CDS data definition

  1. Add some simple logic in the CDS data definition for example to provide 2% discount on actual Price and fill another field say ‘DiscountedPrice’ with the discounted value for all records in the database.

    Note: The logic and examples are mentioned just for your reference and to make the purpose of the challenge easy to understand. Please feel free to use your own logic and dataset.

  2. Now that one of the fields was enhanced, you may need to recreate the draft table ending with _D### before launching the application again. If you do not face any issue you can skip this step.

    To do that, go to the Project Explorer and open the behavior definition click on the icon as shown on the picture to follow the quick fix. Save and activate the draft table.

    Recreate Draft Table

Task 3: Use Joule->Explain capability to understand code

Use ‘Explain’ capability of Joule to get detailed explanation of the code added.

Hint: Please add few lines of code like a simple CASE statement in CDS data definition to test Explain feature.

You may also watch this video to know more about how to use Joule->Explain feature.

We will be back next week with another feature of Joule AI Capability. Stay Tuned!!

entry.title: Data Integration in ABAP Cloud: Data Consumption using External Entities
entry.content:
In the previous post, we discussed data exposure using SQL services. In this post, we shift the focus to the data consumption scenario.

Imagine an ABAP Cloud system that needs to access data located elsewhere, perhaps in an SAP HANA Cloud database, or even in another ABAP system. With SQL Services, an ABAP system can itself act as a database. To support such outbound data access in a clean and cloud-compliant way, ABAP Cloud introduces a new CDS modelling artifact: CDS External Entities.

 

What Are CDS External Entities?

CDS External Entities enable outbound SQL access from an ABAP Cloud system. This means you can:

  • Access data from a remote database
  • Load and process remote data in ABAP
  • Combine remote data with local data

In ABAP Cloud, classic secondary database connections are not available. CDS External Entities are their more powerful and cloud-ready replacement.

Conceptually, a CDS External Entity is comparable to an SAP HANA virtual table; it represents a local view of a remote table or view. The remote object and its fields may have different names, which can be mapped explicitly.

CDS External Entities can be consumed in two different ways:

- Dynamic Access

In the SAP BTP ABAP environment, a CDS External Entity can be used directly in ABAP SQL. You write a SELECT statement that accesses data from a remote database, loads it, and processes it in ABAP.

- Static Access

Starting with release 2508, CDS view entities and classic CDS views can be built on top of external entities. This allows you to integrate remote data seamlessly into your CDS view modelling and treat it like local data. This feature will be used in my next blog post of the series.

Logical External Schema

Alongside CDS External Entities, there is a second important artifact: the Logical External Schema which is based on SAP HANA Smart Data Access (SDA). The key features are as follows:

  • It represents a remote system at design time.
  • It is mapped to a concrete remote source at configuration time.
  • It contains connection-related information (host, port, user, etc.).

From a runtime perspective, the ABAP system sends the query to its own HANA database, which then federates the query to the remote system using SDA.

 

How External Entities Work at Runtime

External entities allow remote data access using a new ABAP SQL syntax:

SELECT FROM <external_entity>

  PROVIDED BY <logical_external_schema>

  INTO TABLE @DATA(result).

In this flow:

  1. The ABAP application sends the SQL query to the local HANA database.
  2. HANA performs query federation using Smart Data Access.
  3. Data is retrieved from the remote database.
  4. Results are returned to the ABAP application server.

The <logical_external_schema> is a design-time artifact that points to the remote system and is bound to an actual remote source during configuration.

 

Example Scenario

Let us build an example based on the travel scenario in which

  • SAP HANA Cloud acts as the remote database.
  • The ABAP system consumes data via Dynamic CDS External Entities.

It is crucial that

  • The external entity has the same name and field structure as the remote HANA table.
  • Field names and types must match exactly (HANA is case-sensitive).

Besides, to enable the access, an outbound service and a communication scenario are required.

Design Time Steps

1. Define a Dynamic CDS External Entity

@AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #CHECK
@EndUserText.label: 'external entity for travel'
define external entity ZI_EE_TRAVEL_000 external name TRAVEL
{
  key TravelID      : abap.numc(8)                external name "TravelID";
      AgencyID      : abap.numc(6)                external name AgencyID;
      CustomerID    : abap.numc(6)                external name CustomerID;
      @Semantics.amount.currencyCode: 'CurrencyCode'
      BookingFee    : abap.curr(16,2)             external name BookingFee;
      @Semantics.amount.currencyCode: 'CurrencyCode'
      TotalPrice    : abap.curr(16,2)             external name TotalPrice;
      CurrencyCode  : abap.cuky                   external name CurrencyCode;
      BeginDate     : abap.dats                   external name BeginDate;
      EndDate       : abap.dats                   external name EndDate;
      Description   : abap.sstring(1024)          external name Description;
      OverallStatus : abap.char(1)                external name OverallStatus;
      CreatedBy     : abp_creation_user           external name CreatedBy;
      CreatedAt     : abp_creation_tstmpl         external name CreatedAt;
      LastChangedBy : abp_locinst_lastchange_user external name LastChangedBy;
}
with federated data 
provided at runtime;

This entity models the structure of the remote table or view.

2. Define a Logical External Schema

This acts as a placeholder for the remote database and is referenced by the external entity.

3. Create Outbound Service and Communication Scenario

  • Create an SQL type outbound service for the logical external schema
  • Create a communication scenario and assign the outbound service to it. Then save and publish the scenario locally.

This enables administrators to configure access to the remote system

4. Consume the External Entity in ABAP SQL

Create a class and write ABAP SQL code

Select * from my external entity provided by my remote into table my local table

CLASS zcl_travel_consume_000 DEFINITION
  PUBLIC
  FINAL
  CREATE PUBLIC .

  PUBLIC SECTION.
    INTERFACES if_oo_adt_classrun .
  PROTECTED SECTION.
  PRIVATE SECTION.
ENDCLASS.

CLASS zcl_travel_consume_000 IMPLEMENTATION.
METHOD if_oo_adt_classrun~main.
     data: travel type table of zi_ee_travel_000.

    select * from zi_ee_travel_000 provided by z_travel_remote_db_000 into table @Travel.

    out->write( travel ).
    endmethod.

ENDCLASS.

In summary, what happens at runtime is as follows

  • The ABAP system sends the query to its HANA database
  • HANA SDA forwards the query to the remote database
  • Data is fetched and returned to the ABAP application
  • The local internal table is filled with remote data

Now we need to establish the connection between our development system and the SAP HANA Cloud database.

 

Configuration Time Steps

1. Create Communication Arrangement

In the SAP Fiori Launchpad:

  • Create a communication arrangement for the communication scenario you published in design time.

2. Maintain a communication system for the remote database

  • Activate Remote SQL-Access
  • Choose HANA ODBC as the SDA adapter
  • Provide host, port, driver, and additional connection settings
    (These details are typically provided by the system administrator)

save the communication system.

3. Maintain Remote Schema Information

In the communication arrangement:

  • Specify the schema name of the remote HANA database
  • Save the configuration and check whether the connection is successful

4. Prepare the Remote HANA Database

In Visual Studio Code and using SAP HANA Database Explorer:

  • Create a table that matches the CDS External Entity exactly
  • Ensure field names, types, and sizes match as well

Remember that SAP HANA is case-sensitive.

What Happens Behind the Scenes

When you execute the ABAP SQL statement:

  • The ABAP SQL layer resolves the external entity and logical external schema
  • HANA automatically:
    • Creates a remote source
    • Creates the required virtual table
  • Query federation is handled transparently

In this sense, CDS External Entities act as a smart abstraction that automatically manages HANA remote sources and virtual tables via ABAP design-time objects.

Press F9, and the data is returned as expected.

In the next blog post I would like to build a RAP application in the cloud, based on data I read from an on-premises system. So, stay tuned!

entry.title: Build Partner Add-on with IDE Action
entry.content:
Disclaimer: The namespace /ITAPC3/ is used throughout this document as an example namespace only. Replace it with your own namespace in real implementations. The coding style/naming conventions used in this blog are not recommendations on naming/design 
  
This blog post covers a step-by-step guide to how partners implement IDE Actions as Partner Add-ons in the SAP BTP ABAP Environment. This enables SAP partners to deliver customized tools in the form of custom action in ABAP Development Tools that increase developer productivity. By combining IDE Actions with the installable add-on option of the BTP ABAP Environment, partners have a potentially monetizable option to extend the ABAP Developer tools. It covers the key concepts and technologies – how to extend ABAP Development Tools with IDE Actions and how to leverage the SAP Business Technology Platform ABAP Environment alongside the Landscape Portal to deliver installable add-on products. 

Here we will look into a partner persona, where they can provide the business solution to their consumer with an installable add-on by providing certain action in the ABAP development tools, and also look into the consumer side of how they can consume the solution which caters to the needs of their developer using ABAP development tools (ADT). 

Why IDE actions and Installable add-ons? 

By combining IDE actions and installable add-ons, partners who have been extending software can now also extend the ABAP Development Tools (ADT). 

  • The IDE action framework allows extending the ABAP development tools (ADT) with custom server-driven context-aware actions. IDE Actions allow partners to automate repetitive tasks, such as creating specialized development objects, enhancing code analysis, or automating some code generation within ABAP Development Tools (ADT) for Eclipse. The implementation is completely server-driven using ABAP only. You can also use these custom functionalities with AI integration and XCO libraries. 
  • Installable add-ons are a set of software components bundled together as an add-on by a partner with the intent of providing a solution to their customers.  
  • The Landscape Portal acts as a central tool to allow service providers to perform administration tasks such as hibernating systems, creating test tenants and support users, selecting systems for a pre-upgrade, but also lifecycle management operations such as building, deploying and updating product versions, and even guides you through the configuration and deployment of multitenant SaaS solutions. 

 Business scenario: 

The developer can document development objects in the Knowledge Transfer Document Editor using Markdown syntax. Here partner is offering a translation action with the help of IDE Action as Add-on for the Knowledge Transfer Document and can use the ABAP AI SDK powered by Intelligent Scenario Lifecycle Management (ISLM) to translate the Markdown syntax and display the output of translated Knowledge Transfer Document. Consumer (The partner's customer) can consume this solution from the partner through landscape portal as Installable add-on and can use customized action in ABAP Development Tools for their development scenario. 

Prerequisites: (Partner) 

  • You’ve purchased entitlements that are necessary for the account setup. See Prepare 
  • You’ve registered a namespace at SAP, for example /NAMESPC/. See Register a Namespace 
  • You’ve registered your add-on at SAP, for example /NAMESPC/PRODUCTX. See Register a Product 
  • You are subscribed to the Landscape Portal 
  • You have a user with the Landscape Portal Admin role collection assigned 
  • Install ABAP development tools for Eclipse (ADT) on your computer. If you already have ADT, make sure it's updated to the latest version. For more information, see ABAP Development Tools. 
  • You need to have access to an SAP BTP ABAP Environment Instance 
  • Business Catalog SAP_A4C_BC_DEV_AIA_PC required for creating and editing IDE actions 

 Prerequisites: (Consumer) 

  • The Landscape Portal Application has been subscribed to and your user has the landscape portal admin role collection assigned 
  • Install ABAP development tools for Eclipse (ADT) on your computer. If you already have ADT, make sure it's updated to the latest version. For more information, see ABAP Development Tools. 

 Steps to Implement as a Partner 

1. Create Software Components 

  • To open the "Manage Software Components" application, log in to your SAP BTP ABAP Environment Fiori Launchpad as an administrator and Search for "Manage Software Components" in the search bar. 
  • use the “Create” button in the “Manage Software Components” app to open the dialog and fill in the information. We enter the name of the software component and a description. In the namespace dropdown you can use your reserved namespace. (Disclaimer: /ITAPC3/ being an example namespace used in this blog) 

  • You clone the software component into the development system. When cloning, the main branch is checked out for the system, a package is created in the system that has the same name as the software component and is also assigned to it. At the same time, you can also set how the clone of the repository behaves in the system (push/pull or just pull). 
  • The software component with the selected branch is now imported into your current system instance. The Clone button turns into a Pull button. From now on, you can use it to pull (remotely available) changes of your software component to your system. Now you can see package under the selected software component in your system. 

2.  Development (Intelligent Scenario) 

Here in this scenario, we would be using ABAP AI Capabilities is our IDE Action implementation class to translate the markdown content into the target language with the help of ABAP AI SDK powered by ISLM. To learn more about Developing AI Enabled Applications with ABAP AI SDK powered by ISLM, refer to the blog post. 

2.1 Create ISLM Scenario: 

  • Select your package, right-click, New → Other → Intelligent Scenario → /ITAPC3/ADT_INT_SCE 

Enter a name and description. Select SIDEBYSIDE for Scenario Technology field. Choose Next and then click Finish. 

2.2 Create ISLM Model: 

  • Select your package, right-click, New → Other → Intelligent Scenario Model 
  • Fill the Description and Model Name fields. Assign the previously created scenario in Intelligent Scenario Name. Choose Next.  

  • Enter the ExecutableID and Large Language Model Name and Large Language Model Version and Activate the object. 

3.  Development (IDE Action) 

In this section we will look at the IDE action which translates the markdown content into the selected language with the help of intelligent scenario created in the previous step. 

Partners can utilize the ABAP AI SDK powered by ISLM to embed Generative AI features in IDE action implementation class. 

  • From ABAP Development Tools, locate and add to the favorite package “/ITAPC3/IDE_ACTION_EXTENSIONS“ that was created during Create Software Components process. The package has the same name as the software component. 
  • Create a new IDE action either from the main menu, project explorer, or toolbar. 
  • In the main menu, choose File->New->Other... and select IDE Action. The creation wizard opens. 
  • In the creation wizard, enter the project and package details if they are not already filled. Also, provide a name and description for the IDE action. 
  • Choose Next, assign a transport request if needed and Choose Finish. 

  • After we have created the action in the system, we then fill the form with the relevant information to create the action and the Input UI Configuration Class. In the Filter: Cardinality section, decide the number of focused resources that can be selected when running an IDE action. If you want to use your IDE action for any object type, add *ALL as value for the object type. Save and activate your IDE action. 

 

3.1 Defining UI 

  • In the first step we need an action dialog to enable the user to enter data. To do this we can create a new class using the link in the IDE Action. This implements the interface IF_AIA_SD_ACTION_INPUT, but only the method CREATE_INPUT_CONFIG is defined here; the other three methods can be implemented optionally. We define a public type in the class as the structure for the input; we can give the fields certain properties using the ABAP Docs. A list of properties can be found in the documentation, with certain properties being set via configuration. 

 

CLASS /itapc3/cl_trans_ui_info DEFINITION 
  PUBLIC 
  FINAL 
  CREATE PUBLIC . 
  PUBLIC SECTION. 
  INTERFACES if_aia_sd_action_input. 
     "! <p class="shorttext">Simple Controls</p> 
      BEGIN OF ty_input, 
      "! <p class="shorttext">Select the language</p> 
      custom_value_help_text_field TYPE string, 
      "! <p class="shorttext">Enter the markdown source for the translation</p> 
        multiline_text_field         TYPE string, 
      END OF ty_input. 
  PROTECTED SECTION. 
  PRIVATE SECTION. 
ENDCLASS. 

CLASS /ITAPC3/CL_TRANS_UI_INFO IMPLEMENTATION. 
  METHOD if_aia_sd_action_input~create_input_config. 
  " Create the input data with default values 
  DATA(input_data) = VALUE ty_input( ) ##NO_TEXT. 
  " Create the configuration via the factory 
  DATA(configuration) = ui_information_factory>get_configuration_factory( 
                                       )->create_for_data( input_data ). 
  " Set the layout 
  configuration->set_layout( if_sd_config_element=>layout-grid ). 
  " Set configuration for elements 
  “To enable a custom value help for an element, the corresponding configuration must be set via the API: 
   configuration->get_element( 'custom_value_help_text_field' )->set_values( if_sd_config_element=>values_kind-domain_specific_named_items ). 
    configuration->get_element( 'multiline_text_field' )->set_multiline( if_sd_config_element=>height-large ). 
  " Create and return the UI information 
 result = ui_information_factory->for_abap_type( abap_type     = input_data 
             configuration = configuration ). 
 ENDMETHOD. 
 METHOD if_aia_sd_action_input~get_value_help_provider. 
  " Return a reference to your value help handler via the value help provider 
   result = cl_sd_value_help_provider=>create( NEW /ITAPC3/CL_TRAN_VALUE_HELP( ) ). 
  ENDMETHOD. 
ENDCLASS.

 

3.2 Implement a Custom Value Help: 

  • To implement a custom value help that returns domain-specific named items, a handler class must be provided that implements the if_sd_value_help_dsni interface. The get_value_help_items method returns the values for the value help. The values to be returned are of type if_sd_value_help_dsni=>ty_named_item_list. 

 

CLASS /itapc3/cl_trans_ide_action DEFINITION 
  PUBLIC 
  FINAL 
  CREATE PUBLIC. 
  PUBLIC SECTION. 
  INTERFACES if_aia_action. 
  PROTECTED SECTION. 
  PRIVATE SECTION. 
  METHODS get_input_as_html 
      IMPORTING ui_input      TYPE /itapc3/cl_trans_ui_info=>ty_input 
      RETURNING VALUE(result) TYPE string. 
ENDCLASS. 

CLASS /ITAPC3/CL_TRANS_IDE_ACTION IMPLEMENTATION. 
 METHOD get_input_as_html. 
    FINAL(nl) = cl_abap_char_utilities=>newline. 
    TRY. 
        DATA(completion_api) = cl_aic_islm_compl_api_factory=>get( )->create_instance( '/ITAPC3/ADT_INT_SCE' ). 
        DATA(prompt) = |You are a translator. Reject Hate Speech. Translate the given text into { ui_input-custom_value_help_text_field }:{ nl }{ ui_input-multiline_text_field }| ##NO_TEXT. 
        DATA(translation) = completion_api->execute_for_string( prompt )->get_completion( ). 
      CATCH cx_aic_api_factory INTO DATA(aic_api_factory_ex). 
      RETURN. 
      CATCH cx_aic_completion_api INTO DATA(aic_completion_api_ex). 
      RETURN. 
    ENDTRY. 
    result =  '<h3 style="color:gray">Translate to (' && ui_input-custom_value_help_text_field && ')</h3>' && nl  && '<pre>' && translation && '</pre>'. 
  ENDMETHOD. 

  METHOD if_aia_action~run. 
  DATA(focused_resource) = context->get_focused_resource( ). 
    TRY. 
        DATA ui_input TYPE /itapc3/cl_trans_ui_info=>ty_input. 
        context->get_input_config_content( )->get_as_structure( IMPORTING result = ui_input ). 
      CATCH cx_sd_invalid_data INTO DATA(exception). 
        CLEAR ui_input. 
    ENDTRY. 
    DATA(popup_result) = cl_aia_result_factory=>create_html_popup_result( ). 
    popup_result->set_content( get_input_as_html( ui_input ) ). 
    result = popup_result. 
  ENDMETHOD. 
ENDCLASS.

 

To start the action in Eclipse, we can copy the markdown source from any KTD Editor and use  use the shortcut Ctrl + Alt + R for Windows or CMD + OPT + R for Mac or choose Run->ABAP IDE Action... from the main menu to start the Action Dialog, where our IDE Action should now be available. 

Once all the required objects are created and the action is working as per our expectation, we have to release the Transport Request. 

4.  Create Add-on Product using Landscape Portal 

The Landscape Portal acts as a central tool to allow service providers to perform administration tasks such as hibernating systems, creating test tenants and support users, selecting systems for a pre-upgrade, but also lifecycle management operations such as building, deploying and updating product versions, and even guides you through the configuration and deployment of multitenant SaaS solutions. 

Here partner can create a new Add-on product, register your product, build a product version via pipelines and deploy a product version. 

4.1 Register Product 

A product refers to an ABAP add-on that can be installed on SAP BTP, ABAP environment. 

  • Log into the Landscape Portal. For more information, see Landscape Portal   
  • Click on the Register Product tile to open the app. 
  • A list of all your created products, including their product ID, namespace and registration status, is displayed. The registration status indicates whether a product has already been registered to the global account you are currently logged in to. 
  • Click the Create button in the top right corner. 
  • Enter the name of the product you want to create and confirm with Create. Your request for creation has been sent and the registration status of the product changes to “Pending…”. The namespace is automatically determined from the product name you entered, and the product ID is generated as part of the creation process. 

 4.2 Build Product Version 

You can use the Build Product Version app to easily trigger the build of new product versions via pipelines based on templates that you can configure for different use cases such as new (test or production) release deliveries, (test or production) support packages or (test or production) hotfixes/patch deliveries. 

  • Log in to the Landscape Portal and click on the tile Build Product Version. For more information, see Build Product Version 
  • Select one of your products from the list. You will be forwarded to the Product Versions tab. Here, you can see a table listing all the existing versions of this product, their type, when they were created, as well as their delivery status. 
  • You also need to Configure a Pipeline Template for release build. For more information, see Configure a Pipeline Template and Create a template for release build.  
  • To create a new product version, click the Create button, choose Release Delivery and select the desired type. The resulting product version will be calculated automatically. 
  • Select software components to be included in your new product version. To add additional software components, click the Add button on the right. For each software component, define a component version, enter the desired branch and commit ID as seen in the Manage Software Components app, and select in which languages your product version should be built. To get relevant commit ID for the software component involved in the build, you can refer the help page to get more details on this. Identify the commit based on which you wish to build your add-on product. 

  • Click the Build Product Version button to trigger the build of the pipeline for your new product version. 
  • You will be redirected to the Pipeline Status screen, where you can track the progress of your build. 

4.3 Check Product Version 

The Check Product Version app lets you view the results of product version delivery infrastructure checks to see if your product version is ready for delivery.  

  • Click on the Check Product Version tile to open the app. For more information, see Check Product Version 
  • A list of all your products is displayed. Select one of the products from the list. 
  • Ready for production (P): The product version is ready for production use. 

4.4 Publish Product 

You, as a partner, have developed a product that you want to make available to your customers as an add-on that they can install into their system. You can use the Publish Product app to publish your product in a specific version for use by customers. Your customer can then use the Discover Product app to view all product versions that were published, i.e. made available, for them and can register a product, thus adding a product to their pool of product versions available for deployment. 

  1. Log into the Landscape Portal. 
  1. Click the Publish Product tile to open the app. For more information, see Publish Product 
  1. Select the product from the list. You are forwarded to the configuration page. Click the Edit button. 
  1. In the Product Configuration section, select the Product Version that you want to publish and choose SAP Store as the Distribution Channel. 
  1. Click the Publish button to publish your product version 

 

Steps performed from the Consumer 

1. Discover Product 

  • Once Partner has made their product available and has published a product version using the Publish Product app, customers can register the product via Discover Product app. 
  • After registrations customers can deploy the product using Deploy Product.  

2.  Deploy Product 

You can use the Deploy Product app to deploy or update your product version in specific systems. 

  • Sign in to the Landscape Portal. 
  • Click on the Deploy Product tile to open the app. 
  • On the left side, under Product and Product Version Selection, select which product and version you want to deploy or update from the drop-down menu. 
  • In the Available Systems section, select at least one system in which you want to deploy or update the product version. You can also select multiple systems at once. 
  • Click the Deploy button. A dialog box will open. Fill in the remaining details: 
  • Title: Choose a title for your update. 
  • From: Select the time the update can begin. 
  • To: The estimated time at which the product version update will be completed. 
  • Confirm with Schedule. 
  • Check the list under Product Version Update Status. Here, all update requests as well as their current status and progress are displayed. The update you just scheduled has been added to the list. 
  • Once the product is deployed successfully then Customer can access the product in target system. 

SAP BTP automatically activates the software component provided from the partner in the ABAP environment, makes packages visible and registers IDE extensions. 

3.  Test IDE Action 

Create/Use ABAP Cloud project in the ABAP instance where you have deployed the product.This should be a development system. 

Open any Knowledge Transfer Document in the editor and copy the content. 

To open the Run ABAP IDE Action dialog, use the shortcut Ctrl + Alt + R for Windows or CMD + OPT + R for Mac or choose Run->ABAP IDE Action... from the main menu. 

Select the IDE action “Translate markdown content” you would like to run Select Run. 

It will open the action input dialog where you can select the target language you want to translate and in next text box area provide the Knowledge Transfer Document content. 

Select Run. 

The result dialog for the selected IDE action opens the result dialog where it translates the Knowledge Transfer Document to the selected language option from the input dialog.

entry.title: Week 3 : Use RAP Business Logic Prediction to Implement Validation
entry.content:
Last week we had created a RAP Application and added some entries in it. We also explored how to use Joule's 'Explain' feature to understand a simple code added in the CDS Data Definition. 

This week let us go one step further and explore another very interesting feature of Joule's ABAP AI capability -  The RAP Business Logic Prediction.

Quick Links

Please use this separate thread to ask your questions and discuss issues. 

Sample Scenario

Here let us think about any simple scenario like changing a field to ‘Mandatory’ and then create a new entry without filling value for this field. Empty values should not be accepted for the mandatory field and we can use the ‘validation’ keyword to check if its kept empty. Here use Joule’s ‘RAP Predict Business Logic’ capability to suggest the code to raise an error message in the implementation method of the validation function. Let’s see the steps in detail.

Task1: Create a validation and implement the corresponding method.

  1. Create a validation in the BDEF of the CDS view. Open your behavior definition and specify one of the fields as mandatory.Define Mandatory Field
  2. Define the Validation in same BDEF of the CDS view.Validation Defenition
  3. In order to have draft instances being checked by validations before they become active, they have to be specified for the draft determine action prepare in the behavior definition. Replace the code line draft determine action Prepare; in your BDEF with the following code snippet as shown on the screenshot below.Prepare Action
  4. Save and activate the changes.
  5. Now you can notice a small icon to the left of the validation definition. Click on it to create the implementation method for adding validation logic in the local handler class of the behavior pool of your entity. Save and activate the changes. Hint: You can right click on the new method name and choose Quick Fix as well.Implement ValidationFor additional information on Validations and its syntax and use you can refer the BDEF ‘/DMO/R_Travel_D’. Alternatively you can also refer https://github.com/SAP-samples/abap-platform-rap100/tree/main/exercises/ex05

Task2: Enhance validation with RAP Predict Business Logic

Use Joule's RAP Predict Business Logic to add the logic to check whether the mandatory field created in Task 1 is filled while creating a new entry. If it is left empty throw an error message.

Note:- The logic and examples are mentioned just for your reference and to make the purpose of the challenge easy to understand. Please feel free to use your own logic and dataset.

To know how to use RAP Predict Business Logic follow this tutorial. Hint: You can right click on the new method name and choose Quick Fix as well.

Good luck and wait for the last challenge next week. Until then Stay Jouled !!!

entry.title: Oops we did it again! A brand new top-level ABAP keyword in ABAP CE 2602.
entry.content:
Introduction

New ABAP commands arise only once in most people's lifetimes. But after the ABAP command WITH in AS ABAP 7.51, we have now introduced the new ABAP command MERGE.
The fact that both keywords concern databases is no coincidence. Leveraging the full power of the HANA database is one of ABAP's highest priorities.
Note that we use "command" as a synonym for top-level keywords in this article.

Introducing a new ABAP top-level keyword in the 21st century

What is the problem with introducing a new ABAP keyword? Macros. Consider the following coding:

DEFINE merge.
  WRITE : / &1.
END-OF-DEFINITION. 
 
MERGE 'hello'.

 

After introducing MERGE as a new keyword, this code would have syntax errors. This is why, for example, RAP avoided introducing any new top-level keyword with EML. MODIFY, READ, COMMIT all existed well before RAP. 
But the developers of ABAP SQL are not afraid, as they have already shown in the past. With MERGE, they introduced the concept of a weak ABAP keyword! Macros take precedence. Here is what happens when you use a macro named MERGE in 2602:

So despite a number of warnings, nothing bad happens. You cannot use the new ABAP command MERGE in this program, though. There is and will be no escape mechanism. 

Of course, macros are not available in ABAP Cloud and this is only relevant for customers when MERGE makes it into the next On Premise release.

What does MERGE do?

MERGE is a standard SQL database command. It exists on the HANA database and most other commercial databases. Practically it is a more powerful version of UPDATE, DELETE, INSERT and MODIFY all in one command.

As MERGE is an SQL standard keyword and ABAP is a superset of SQL by tradition, we also opted to keep the command name and thus introduce a new ABAP command. 

Conceptually, MERGE is a very easy operation to understand. Here is the flow:

Here, "when no join partner found" means that the joined table (e.g., internal table) contains a set not contained in the database table. The other direction is not covered yet (see below).

 

A practical example

In this section, we give a very easy example of the MERGE command. Say you have a database table of users, and from time to time, you update it from an Excel spreadsheet stored in an internal table.  

A simple merge command would look like follows:

MERGE INTO users
  USING @Excel AS excel
  ON users~userid = excel~userid
  WHEN MATCHED AND users~managed_by_excel = @abap_true THEN UPDATE SET user_data = excel~user_data
  WHEN NOT MATCHED THEN INSERT ALL.

 This command is really self-explanatory. When you want to change the fields inserted into the database, you can also specify them as expressions in the VALUES ( .... ) clause. The usage of DELETE is fairly similar.

Some databases (for example SQL Server) have an additional clause WHEN NOT MATCHED  BY SOURCE, which is triggered when an entry is found in the joined table (e.g. excel) but not in the target table (e.g. users).  This is neither present in ABAP nor on the HANA database yet. 
Thus, conceptually, the join equals a RIGHT OUTER JOIN.

Summary

This article introduces the new ABAP command MERGE, a powerful tool for data manipulation. Due to its ability to join an internal or database table (or a global temporary table for that matter) it can be a very powerful tool in the hands of an ABAP developer.

You may find additional information in the SAP help article here.
