entry.title: How to create a RAP application using a parameterized CDS view
entry.content:
Hello everyone, recently I came across a somewhat unusual requirement where I needed to use a parameter in a RAP application. As we know, this isn’t natively supported with Fiori Elements, so I had to look for a way to make it work. After some research and a few tests, I ended up with a solution that I consider quite innovative: using a Fiori Freestyle approach.

The best part is that it requires only a minimal amount of code, something that any ABAP developer can implement without much effort, and it becomes extremely useful whenever you need to work with parameters that are not directly linked to the data exposed by the CDS view.

The idea behind this solution is simple:

Since projection views in RAP do not support CDS parameters directly, we expose a parameterized interface CDS view through a service definition and service binding.

Then, using a Fiori Freestyle application with a Smart Table, we dynamically inject the parameter into the table binding path before the data request is sent to the backend.

This allows the RAP service to consume the parameterized CDS view without requiring complex workarounds.

 

Let’s go through the required steps:

 

1)  Since it’s not possible to add WITH PARAMETERS to a projection view, we need to create an interface CDS view and use it as our “projection.”

@AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #NOT_REQUIRED
@EndUserText.label: '<your cds description>'
@Metadata.ignorePropagatedAnnotations: false
@Metadata.allowExtensions: true

define view entity <your_parameterized_interface_cds>
  with parameters
    p_date : abap.char(8)

as select from <your_source_cds_or_table>
(
  p_date : $parameters.p_date
)
{
    key <your_key_field>,
        <your_field_1>,
        <your_field_2>,
        <your_field_3>
}

2) Create our service definition normally

3) Create Service Binding ( Odata V2 )

4) Now we have to create the freestyle app with the fiori generator application

5) Now just connect to the S4 and select the service binding created earlier

At this point, you can see the project structure in the Explorer panel on the left. We’ll focus only on the view and controller folders.

 

 

 

6)

First, we need to create our Smart Table in the view folder. Inside the SmartFilterBar, we must set the entitySet from our service binding. After that, we add a customControl in my case, I used a DatePicker, but you can use any control that fits your requirement. I also attached an onDateChange event to the DatePicker so we can capture and manipulate the selected date whenever it changes.

Within the Smart Table, we also need to define the same entitySet. Another very important point here is the beforeRebindTable event. This is the hook that allows us to pass the parameter to the CDS view. It will trigger the onBeforeRebindTable method in the controller, where we can dynamically inject the parameter into the binding before the data is requested.

 

<mvc:View
    xmlns:table="sap.ui.table"
    xmlns="sap.ui.comp.smartfilterbar"
    xmlns:f="sap.f"
    xmlns:m="sap.m"
    xmlns:mvc="sap.ui.core.mvc"
    xmlns:smartTable="sap.ui.comp.smarttable"
    controllerName="<your.app.namespace>.controller.Main"
    height="100%">

<f:DynamicPage
    id="dynamicPage"
    fitContent="true"
    title="{i18n>appTitle}">

<f:header>
<f:DynamicPageHeader>

<SmartFilterBar
    id="smartFilterBar"
    entitySet="<your_entity_set>">

    <controlConfiguration>

        <ControlConfiguration
            key="<your_parameter_field>"
            visibleInAdvancedArea="true"
            label="<your_filter_label>"
            mandatory="true">

            <customControl>

                <m:DatePicker
                    id="datePicker"
                    displayFormat="dd/MM/yyyy"
                    valueFormat="yyyyMMdd"
                    placeholder="dd/mm/yyyy"
                    width="100%"
                    required="true"
                    change="onDateChange"/>

            </customControl>

        </ControlConfiguration>

    </controlConfiguration>

</SmartFilterBar>

</f:DynamicPageHeader>
</f:header>

<f:content>

<smartTable:SmartTable
    id="smartTable"
    beforeRebindTable="onBeforeRebindTable"
    showRowCount="true"
    enableAutoBinding="false"
    entitySet="<your_entity_set>"
    smartFilterId="smartFilterBar"
    useVariantManagement="true"
    tableType="Table"
    initiallyVisibleFields="<your_fields>"
    enableAutoColumnWidth="true"
    showTablePersonalisation="true">

    <smartTable:customToolbar>

        <m:OverflowToolbar design="Transparent">
            <m:ToolbarSpacer/>
        </m:OverflowToolbar>

    </smartTable:customToolbar>

</smartTable:SmartTable>

</f:content>

</f:DynamicPage>

</mvc:View>

7) In this step, we implement the logic inside the onBeforeRebindTable method, which is responsible for dynamically passing the parameter to the CDS view before the Smart Table triggers the data request.

First, the code retrieves the value entered in the DatePicker using byId. This value will be used as the parameter for the CDS. A local variable (sDate) is initialized to store the selected date.

Next, a validation is performed to ensure that the date field is filled in. Since this parameter is mandatory for the query, if no value is provided the DatePicker is set to the Error state and a validation message is displayed. At the same time, the table binding is stopped using:

oBindingParams.preventTableBind = true;

This prevents the Smart Table from calling the backend without the required parameter, avoiding unnecessary requests and possible errors.

If the date is correctly filled in, any previous error state is cleared by setting the ValueState back to None.

Finally, the most important part happens: the table binding path is updated dynamically. Here we pass the selected date to the parameterized CDS by changing the binding path of the Smart Table:

setTableBindingPath("/<EntitySet>(p_date='${sDate}')/Set")

This is what allows the RAP application to consume a CDS with parameters, because the parameter is injected at runtime just before the data is requested from the backend.

onBeforeRebindTable: function (oEvent) {

    let oDatePicker = this.byId("datePicker");
    let sDate = "";

    if (oDatePicker) {
        sDate = oDatePicker.getValue();
    }

    if (!sDate) {

        if (oDatePicker) {
            oDatePicker.setValueState("Error");
            oDatePicker.setValueStateText("Please inform the date.");
        }

        let oBindingParams = oEvent.getParameter("bindingParams");

        if (oBindingParams) {
            oBindingParams.preventTableBind = true;
        }

        return;
    }

    if (oDatePicker) {
        oDatePicker.setValueState("None");
    }

    this.getView()
        .byId("smartTable")
        .setTableBindingPath("/<your_entity_set>(p_date='" + sDate + "')/Set");

}

And Vooooalá!

Conclusion

Although RAP and Fiori Elements provide powerful tools for building enterprise applications, there are still scenarios where certain features are not directly supported, such as consuming parameterized CDS views in projection layers.

By combining RAP services with a lightweight Fiori Freestyle approach, we can overcome this limitation in a simple and flexible way. Using events like beforeRebindTable and dynamically modifying the binding path allows parameters to be passed to the CDS view at runtime.

This approach requires only a small amount of additional UI code and can be implemented quickly by ABAP developers who are already familiar with RAP and Fiori.

I hope this solution helps others facing similar challenges when working with parameterized CDS views in RAP applications.

entry.title: Visual Filters in SAP Fiori Elements: A Complete Guide
entry.content:
Introduction :  SAP Fiori Elements provides several smart UI features that help users interact with business data more efficiently. One such feature is the Visual Filter, which offers a graphical way to filter data in applications like the Analytical List Page. Instead of entering filter values manually, users can select values directly from charts or graphical elements, making the filtering process faster and more intuitive. Visual Filters enhance the user experience by combining data visualization with filtering capabilities, allowing users to quickly analyze data and refine their search results.

Visual Filter:

A Visual Filter in SAP Fiori Elements is a graphical filter control used in the Filter Bar of applications like  Analytical List Page.
Instead of typing filter values manually, users can select values directly from a chart (bar, line, or donut) to filter the data.

A Visual Filter is a small interactive chart in the filter bar that allows users to filter data visually.

-----> Visual Filter = Chart + Filter

It shows aggregated data in a small chart, and when the user clicks a value, the application filters the table data automatically.

Example:

Month_YearNet Value

MAY-20231930.7
SEP-2022254.6
FEB-2023245.5

When a user clicks FEB-2023 (245.5) in the Visual Filter, the application automatically filters the data and displays the corresponding results in the chart and list. Please  find the below Screen shot for your Reference.

 

How to Create Visual Filter  in  BTP BAS:

  1. Open SAP Business Application Studio.

  2. Go to the Explorer panel.

  3. Locate your Fiori project (for example: analytical_report).

  4. Right-click on the project.

  5. Select Open Guided Development.

This option helps developers configure Fiori Elements features

After opening Guided Development:

  1. Select Project Guides.

  2. Choose Analytical List Page.

  3. Scroll down in the options.

  4. Click Add a New Visual Filter to the Filter Bar.

This option is used to create a visual filter chart in the filter bar.

After selecting Add a New Visual Filter to the Filter Bar, a configuration wizard will open.

You will see these tabs:

  • About – Information about the visual filter feature

  • Step 1 – Select entity set and dimension

  • Step 2 – Select measure and chart type

  • Step 3 – Generate the annotation and save

Once the configuration is completed, BAS automatically generates the required CDS/UI annotations.

Please find the Below Screen shot for Your Reference:

Step1:

  <Annotation Term="UI.Chart" Qualifier="Step1_CQ">
                    <Record Type="UI.ChartDefinitionType">
                        <PropertyValue Property="ChartType" EnumMember="UI.ChartType/Bar"/>
                        <PropertyValue Property="Title" String="Purchase Order Data"/>
                        <PropertyValue Property="Description" String="Purchase Order Data"/>
                        <PropertyValue Property="Dimensions">
                            <Collection>
                                <PropertyPath>Month_Year</PropertyPath>
                            </Collection>
                        </PropertyValue>
                        <PropertyValue Property="DimensionAttributes">
                            <Collection>
                                <Record Type="UI.ChartDimensionAttributeType">
                                    <PropertyValue Property="Dimension" PropertyPath="Month_Year"/>
                                    <PropertyValue Property="Role" EnumMember="UI.ChartDimensionRoleType/Category"/>
                                </Record>
                            </Collection>
                        </PropertyValue>
                        <PropertyValue Property="Measures">
                            <Collection>
                                <PropertyPath>Amount</PropertyPath>
                            </Collection>
                        </PropertyValue>
                        <PropertyValue Property="MeasureAttributes">
                            <Collection>
                                <Record Type="UI.ChartMeasureAttributeType">
                                    <PropertyValue Property="Measure" PropertyPath="Amount"/>
                                    <PropertyValue Property="Role" EnumMember="UI.ChartMeasureRoleType/Axis1"/>
                                </Record>
                            </Collection>
                        </PropertyValue>
                    </Record>
                </Annotation>

Step2:

 <Annotation Term="UI.PresentationVariant" Qualifier="Step2_pQ">
                    <Record Type="UI.PresentationVariantType">
                        <PropertyValue Property="Visualizations">
                            <Collection>
                                <AnnotationPath>@UI.Chart#Step1_CQ</AnnotationPath>
                            </Collection>
                        </PropertyValue>
                    </Record>
                </Annotation>

Step3:

 <Annotation Term="Common.ValueList">
                    <Record Type="Common.ValueListType">
                        <PropertyValue Property="CollectionPath" String="Ztest_ALP_Report"/>
                        <PropertyValue Property="PresentationVariantQualifier" String="Step2_pQ"/>
                        <PropertyValue Property="Parameters">
                            <Collection>
                                <Record Type="Common.ValueListParameterInOut">
                                    <PropertyValue Property="LocalDataProperty" PropertyPath="Month_Year"/>
                                    <PropertyValue Property="ValueListProperty" String="Month_Year"/>
                                </Record>
                            </Collection>
                        </PropertyValue>
                    </Record>
                </Annotation>

After completing all the steps, the Visual Filter is successfully added to the Analytical List Page. The filter now appears in the filter bar as an interactive chart, allowing users to select values and automatically filter the data displayed in the charts and table.

 Before  add Visual Filter --- > in the Annotation.xml File.

<edmx:Edmx xmlns:edmx="http://docs.oasis-open.org/odata/ns/edmx" Version="4.0">
    <edmx:Reference Uri="https://sap.github.io/odata-vocabularies/vocabularies/Common.xml">
        <edmx:Include Namespace="com.sap.vocabularies.Common.v1" Alias="Common"/>
    </edmx:Reference>
    <edmx:Reference Uri="https://sap.github.io/odata-vocabularies/vocabularies/UI.xml">
        <edmx:Include Namespace="com.sap.vocabularies.UI.v1" Alias="UI"/>
    </edmx:Reference>
    <edmx:Reference Uri="/sap/opu/odata/sap/ZTEST_PURCHASE_DATA_SRV/$metadata">
        <edmx:Include Namespace="ZTEST_PURCHASE_DATA_SRV" Alias="SAP"/>
    </edmx:Reference>
    <edmx:DataServices>
        <Schema xmlns="http://docs.oasis-open.org/odata/ns/edm" Namespace="local">
           
        </Schema>
    </edmx:DataServices>
</edmx:Edmx>

 After  add Visual Filter --- > in the Annotation.xml File.

  <Annotations Target="SAP.Ztest_ALP_ReportType">
                <Annotation Term="UI.Chart" Qualifier="Step1_CQ">
                    <Record Type="UI.ChartDefinitionType">
                        <PropertyValue Property="ChartType" EnumMember="UI.ChartType/Bar"/>
                        <PropertyValue Property="Title" String="Purchase Order Data"/>
                        <PropertyValue Property="Description" String="Purchase Order Data"/>
                        <PropertyValue Property="Dimensions">
                            <Collection>
                                <PropertyPath>Month_Year</PropertyPath>
                            </Collection>
                        </PropertyValue>
                        <PropertyValue Property="DimensionAttributes">
                            <Collection>
                                <Record Type="UI.ChartDimensionAttributeType">
                                    <PropertyValue Property="Dimension" PropertyPath="Month_Year"/>
                                    <PropertyValue Property="Role" EnumMember="UI.ChartDimensionRoleType/Category"/>
                                </Record>
                            </Collection>
                        </PropertyValue>
                        <PropertyValue Property="Measures">
                            <Collection>
                                <PropertyPath>Amount</PropertyPath>
                            </Collection>
                        </PropertyValue>
                        <PropertyValue Property="MeasureAttributes">
                            <Collection>
                                <Record Type="UI.ChartMeasureAttributeType">
                                    <PropertyValue Property="Measure" PropertyPath="Amount"/>
                                    <PropertyValue Property="Role" EnumMember="UI.ChartMeasureRoleType/Axis1"/>
                                </Record>
                            </Collection>
                        </PropertyValue>
                    </Record>
                </Annotation>
                <Annotation Term="UI.PresentationVariant" Qualifier="Step2_pQ">
                    <Record Type="UI.PresentationVariantType">
                        <PropertyValue Property="Visualizations">
                            <Collection>
                                <AnnotationPath>@UI.Chart#Step1_CQ</AnnotationPath>
                            </Collection>
                        </PropertyValue>
                    </Record>
                </Annotation>
            </Annotations>
            <Annotations Target="SAP.Ztest_ALP_ReportType/Month_Year">
                <Annotation Term="Common.ValueList">
                    <Record Type="Common.ValueListType">
                        <PropertyValue Property="CollectionPath" String="Ztest_ALP_Report"/>
                        <PropertyValue Property="PresentationVariantQualifier" String="Step2_pQ"/>
                        <PropertyValue Property="Parameters">
                            <Collection>
                                <Record Type="Common.ValueListParameterInOut">
                                    <PropertyValue Property="LocalDataProperty" PropertyPath="Month_Year"/>
                                    <PropertyValue Property="ValueListProperty" String="Month_Year"/>
                                </Record>
                            </Collection>
                        </PropertyValue>
                    </Record>
                </Annotation>
            </Annotations>

Note: Instead of following the guided development steps, you can also add the required annotations directly in the annotation file based on the client’s requirements. This approach allows developers to configure Visual Filters manually using CDS/UI annotations.

Onces done  all step sucessfuly you wull see visula filer in the  anaytical list page

Complete  Code --> Visula Filter + Analytical List Page

1.CDS View:
---------------
@AbapCatalog.sqlViewName: 'ZALPR'
@AbapCatalog.compiler.compareFilter: true
@AbapCatalog.preserveKey: true
@AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #NOT_REQUIRED
@EndUserText.label: 'ALP Report'
@Metadata.ignorePropagatedAnnotations: true
@Metadata.allowExtensions: true
define view Ztest_ALP_Report
  as select from ztest_sales_tab
{
  key vbeln as SalesDocument,
      vkorg as SalesOrg,
      werks as Plant,
      vtweg as DistrChannel,
      @DefaultAggregation: #SUM
      netwr as Amount,
      waerk as Currency,
      audat as D_Date,
      case substring( audat, 5, 2 )
      when '01' then concat( 'JAN-', substring( audat, 1, 4 ) )
      when '02' then concat( 'FEB-', substring( audat, 1, 4 ) )
      when '03' then concat( 'MAR-', substring( audat, 1, 4 ) )
      when '04' then concat( 'APR-', substring( audat, 1, 4 ) )
      when '05' then concat( 'MAY-', substring( audat, 1, 4 ) )
      when '06' then concat( 'JUN-', substring( audat, 1, 4 ) )
      when '07' then concat( 'JUL-', substring( audat, 1, 4 ) )
      when '08' then concat( 'AUG-', substring( audat, 1, 4 ) )
      when '09' then concat( 'SEP-', substring( audat, 1, 4 ) )
      when '10' then concat( 'OCT-', substring( audat, 1, 4 ) )
      when '11' then concat( 'NOV-', substring( audat, 1, 4 ) )
      when '12' then concat( 'DEC-', substring( audat, 1, 4 ) )

      end   as Month_Year
}
2.Meta data extension View:
---------------------------
@Metadata.layer: #CORE

@UI.chart: [{
    qualifier: 'chartDefault',
    description: 'Purchase Data',
    chartType: #PIE,
    dimensions: ['Month_Year'],
    measures: ['Amount'],
    dimensionAttributes: [{ dimension: 'Month_Year',role: #SERIES }],
    measureAttributes: [{ measure: 'Amount', role: #AXIS_1 }]
    }
   ]

@UI.presentationVariant: [{
    qualifier: 'Default',
    visualizations: [{ type: #AS_CHART ,qualifier: 'chartDefault'}]
   
     
}]



annotate entity Ztest_ALP_Report with
{
  .lineItem: [{ position: 10 }]
  .selectionField: [{ position: 10 }]

  SalesDocument;
  .lineItem: [{ position: 20 }]
  .selectionField: [{ position: 20 }]
  SalesOrg;
  .lineItem: [{ position: 30 }]
  Plant;
  .lineItem: [{ position: 40 }]
  .selectionField: [{ position: 40 }]
  DistrChannel;
  .lineItem: [{ position: 50 }]
  .dataPoint.qualifier: 'Amount'
  Amount;
  .lineItem: [{ position: 60 }]
  Currency;
  .lineItem: [{ position: 70 }]
  D_Date;
  .lineItem: [{ position: 80 }]
  .selectionField: [{ position: 80 }]
  Month_Year;

}

3. Annotation.xml File:
-----------------------
<edmx:Edmx xmlns:edmx="http://docs.oasis-open.org/odata/ns/edmx" Version="4.0">
    <edmx:Reference Uri="https://sap.github.io/odata-vocabularies/vocabularies/Common.xml">
        <edmx:Include Namespace="com.sap.vocabularies.Common.v1" Alias="Common"/>
    </edmx:Reference>
    <edmx:Reference Uri="https://sap.github.io/odata-vocabularies/vocabularies/UI.xml">
        <edmx:Include Namespace="com.sap.vocabularies.UI.v1" Alias="UI"/>
    </edmx:Reference>
    <edmx:Reference Uri="/sap/opu/odata/sap/ZTEST_PURCHASE_DATA_SRV/$metadata">
        <edmx:Include Namespace="ZTEST_PURCHASE_DATA_SRV" Alias="SAP"/>
    </edmx:Reference>
    <edmx:DataServices>
        <Schema xmlns="http://docs.oasis-open.org/odata/ns/edm" Namespace="local">
           
            <Annotations Target="SAP.Ztest_ALP_ReportType">
                <Annotation Term="UI.Chart" Qualifier="Step1_CQ">
                    <Record Type="UI.ChartDefinitionType">
                        <PropertyValue Property="ChartType" EnumMember="UI.ChartType/Bar"/>
                        <PropertyValue Property="Title" String="Purchase Order Data"/>
                        <PropertyValue Property="Description" String="Purchase Order Data"/>
                        <PropertyValue Property="Dimensions">
                            <Collection>
                                <PropertyPath>Month_Year</PropertyPath>
                            </Collection>
                        </PropertyValue>
                        <PropertyValue Property="DimensionAttributes">
                            <Collection>
                                <Record Type="UI.ChartDimensionAttributeType">
                                    <PropertyValue Property="Dimension" PropertyPath="Month_Year"/>
                                    <PropertyValue Property="Role" EnumMember="UI.ChartDimensionRoleType/Category"/>
                                </Record>
                            </Collection>
                        </PropertyValue>
                        <PropertyValue Property="Measures">
                            <Collection>
                                <PropertyPath>Amount</PropertyPath>
                            </Collection>
                        </PropertyValue>
                        <PropertyValue Property="MeasureAttributes">
                            <Collection>
                                <Record Type="UI.ChartMeasureAttributeType">
                                    <PropertyValue Property="Measure" PropertyPath="Amount"/>
                                    <PropertyValue Property="Role" EnumMember="UI.ChartMeasureRoleType/Axis1"/>
                                </Record>
                            </Collection>
                        </PropertyValue>
                    </Record>
                </Annotation>
                <Annotation Term="UI.PresentationVariant" Qualifier="Step2_pQ">
                    <Record Type="UI.PresentationVariantType">
                        <PropertyValue Property="Visualizations">
                            <Collection>
                                <AnnotationPath>@UI.Chart#Step1_CQ</AnnotationPath>
                            </Collection>
                        </PropertyValue>
                    </Record>
                </Annotation>
            </Annotations>
            <Annotations Target="SAP.Ztest_ALP_ReportType/Month_Year">
                <Annotation Term="Common.ValueList">
                    <Record Type="Common.ValueListType">
                        <PropertyValue Property="CollectionPath" String="Ztest_ALP_Report"/>
                        <PropertyValue Property="PresentationVariantQualifier" String="Step2_pQ"/>
                        <PropertyValue Property="Parameters">
                            <Collection>
                                <Record Type="Common.ValueListParameterInOut">
                                    <PropertyValue Property="LocalDataProperty" PropertyPath="Month_Year"/>
                                    <PropertyValue Property="ValueListProperty" String="Month_Year"/>
                                </Record>
                            </Collection>
                        </PropertyValue>
                    </Record>
                </Annotation>
            </Annotations>
           
           
        </Schema>
    </edmx:DataServices>
</edmx:Edmx>


Conclusion:  In conclusion, Visual Filters in SAP Fiori Elements provide an intuitive way to filter and analyze data using graphical charts. They help users quickly understand data trends and apply filters easily, improving both usability and efficiency.

 

Thank You,

B Naresh Kumar Nayak

entry.title: SAP Fiori Elements Full Tutorial in SAP BTP BAS – End-to-End Guide
entry.content:
Introduction: SAP Fiori Elements is a framework that enables developers to build SAP Fiori applications quickly using predefined templates and annotations. Instead of writing large amounts of UI code, developers can define application behavior and UI structure using CDS views and OData services. SAP Fiori Elements automatically generates the user interface based on these annotations, ensuring consistency with SAP Fiori design guidelines.

What is SAP Fiori Elements?

SAP Fiori Elements is a metadata-driven UI framework built on SAPUI5 that automatically generates enterprise-grade SAP Fiori applications .SAP Fiori Elements provides standardized floorplans that cover the majority of enterprise application scenarios.

One Word Definition (Fiori Element)--> SAP Fiori Elements can be described as a standardized floorplan-based application framework.

Fiori Elements---> ( CDS+ Annotation+ Odata services ) .

Types of Fiori Elemnts:
1. List Report – Displays and filters business data in tabular format
2.Object Page – Shows detailed information about a single business object.
3.Analytical List Page (ALP) – KPIs + charts + table
4.Overview Page (OVP) – Dashboard-style layout with multiple cards.
5.Worklist– Displays a list of items requiring action.

Mandatory Components : To develop and run a Fiori Elements application in SAP BTP that consumes an On-Premise SAP system, the following components are mandatory
1. SAP Cloud Connector.
2. SAP Business Application Studio.
3. SAP BTP Trial Account.
4. On-Premise System Details (For SAP S/4HANA or ECC Systems ).

 

Topic: Complete SAP Fiori Elements Learning Series: I have developed and covered all major concepts related to SAP Fiori Elements and BTP integration.

Please go through the link below to learn how to create SAP Fiori Elements applications. The step-by-step process for developing Fiori Elements apps is explained in detail in the following tutorial.

1.How to Connect On-Premise System to BTP BAS : https://community.sap.com/t5/abap-blog-posts/how-to-connect-an-on-premise-abap-system-to-sap-btp-bas-using-sap-cloud/ba-p/14340330 

2.List Reort:  https://community.sap.com/t5/abap-blog-posts/how-to-create-a-list-report-in-sap-fiori-elements-step-by-step-guide/ba-p/14346960 

3.Object Page:  https://community.sap.com/t5/abap-blog-posts/how-to-create-an-object-list-page-in-sap-fiori-elements-step-by-step-guide/ba-p/14347253 

4.Analytical List Page (ALP): https://community.sap.com/t5/abap-blog-posts/how-to-create-an-analytical-list-page-in-sap-fiori-elements-step-by-step/ba-p/14348144 

5.Overview Page (OVP): https://community.sap.com/t5/abap-blog-posts/overview-page-in-sap-fiori-elements-a-step-by-step-guide/ba-p/14348973 

 

Conclusion : In conclusion, SAP Fiori Elements simplifies the development of SAP Fiori applications by using templates and metadata-driven approaches. It reduces development effort, ensures a consistent user experience, and allows developers to focus more on business logic rather than UI coding.

Thank  You,

B Naresh Kumar Nayak

entry.title: How to Create a Define View in SAP CDS
entry.content:
Introduction

In SAP CDS, a Define View is the basic structure used to create a virtual data model from database tables. It allows developers to select and expose specific fields that can be used in reports, OData services, and SAP Fiori applications and RAP Application.

Define View:

  • Define View is the basic syntax used to create a CDS View (entity) in SAP. It defines a virtual data model that selects and exposes fields from one or more database tables.
  • The view does not store data physically; it retrieves data dynamically when it is accessed.

How  to Create  CDS View:

To create a CDS View, open ABAP Development Tools (ADT) in Eclipse .

Please follow Below Steps:  Right click on  Your  Project  and Click on New and Select ABAP Repository Object

After selecting ABAP Repository Object, a new window will appear. In this window, choose Core Data Services, and under it select Data Definition.

The Data Definition option is used to create a CDS View in SAP. After selecting it, click Next to proceed with creating the CDS view.

In Data Definition, you need to enter the required details such as the Package name, CDS View name, and a short description for the CDS view. 

After clicking the Next button, the Transport Request window will appear. Here you need to select the appropriate Transport Request (TR).

In real-time projects, the Transport Request number will be displayed here. Usually, the Transport Request is created first using transactions SE01 or SE09 in SAP GUI. Once the TR is created, it will automatically appear in Eclipse (ADT) when you create a new repository object.Select the required Transport Request and proceed to create the CDS View.

Real Time: Please see the below  screen short for Your Refrence.

After selecting the Transport Request and clicking the Next button, the Templates section will appear.

In this section, select the Define View template under View (creation). After selecting the template, click the Finish button.

Once you click Finish, the system will automatically generate a CDS Define View template, where you can start writing the CDS view code.

Example Define View:

Key Point:

  • Lines starting with @ are called annotations.
  • The  keyword define view is used to define the CDS entity name.
  • The annotation @AbapCatalog.sqlViewName 
  • The keyword as select from  specifies the database table or view.
  • The section between curly braces { }is called the projection list

Code:

@AbapCatalog.sqlViewName: 'ZDVC'
@AbapCatalog.compiler.compareFilter: true
@AbapCatalog.preserveKey: true
@AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #NOT_REQUIRED
@EndUserText.label: 'Define View'
@Metadata.ignorePropagatedAnnotations: true
define view ZNayak_Define_View
  as select from /dmo/travel
{
  key travel_id     as TravelId,
      agency_id     as AgencyId,
      customer_id   as CustomerId,
      begin_date    as BeginDate,
      end_date      as EndDate,
      booking_fee   as BookingFee,
      total_price   as TotalPrice,
      currency_code as CurrencyCode,
      description   as Description,
      status        as Status,
      createdby     as Createdby,
      createdat     as Createdat,
      lastchangedby as Lastchangedby,
      lastchangedat as Lastchangedat
}

Onces  complete  your logic   Activate  and Excute the CDS View.  

Out Put:

Define View Entity:

  • its  same as  Define View.
  • its a new  version view .
  • ABAP 7.55 onwards it is availabe,
  • No need @AbapCatalog.sqlViewName  annotation.
  • erformance is better

A Define View Entity works almost the same as a Define View in SAP CDS. The main difference is that while creating the CDS object, you need to select the Define View Entity template instead of the Define View template.

 

Code:

@AbapCatalog.viewEnhancementCategory: [#NONE]
@AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #NOT_REQUIRED
@EndUserText.label: 'Define View Entity'
@Metadata.ignorePropagatedAnnotations: true
define view entity ZNayak_Define_View_Entity
  as select from /dmo/travel
{
  key travel_id     as TravelId,
      agency_id     as AgencyId,
      customer_id   as CustomerId,
      begin_date    as BeginDate,
      end_date      as EndDate,
      currency_code as CurrencyCode,
      description   as Description,
      status        as Status,
      createdby     as Createdby,
      createdat     as Createdat,
      lastchangedby as Lastchangedby,
      lastchangedat as Lastchangedat

}

OutPut:

Define view and Define  view Entiy  both are same  but  define view entity as some more feature are there. 

 

Conclusion:

The Define View is the basic building block in SAP CDS used to create virtual data models from database tables. It allows developers to select required fields, define keys, and prepare data that can be consumed by applications such as reports, OData services, and SAP Fiori apps.

By using Define Views, developers can implement the Code Pushdown concept, improve system performance, and create reusable data models in modern SAP development.

 

 

Thank You,

B Naresh Kumar Nayak

entry.title: RAP Unmanaged Draft: Early Numbering and Associations for Unique IDs
entry.content:
INTRODUCTION

In RAP, Implementing numbering mechanisms during these operations is essential to ensure unique identifiers for newly created entities.
This blog is exposure the best for numbering during Creation and Creation by Association, in Unmanaged Scenario with Draft. How to handle number creation with Draft Scenario's.

Some Information about Numbering
Basically, Numbering is the concept to process assigning unique values to the key fields of business entities during Create and Create By Association Operations.

Unmanaged Early Numbering:

  • Developers need to implement custom code logic to create and assign keys values during the creation and create by association operation. 
  • %is_draft – It is a component of the transactional key (%tky) in RAP. 
    • It tells the framework whether to operate on the draft table or the active persistent table for a given operation. 
    • %is_draft = mk-on reads from draft table 
    • %is_draft = mk-off reads from active table 

Problem Statement

A sales clerk begins creating a Sales Order in Fiori. The order header is saved as a draft, but items need to be added immediately under that draft. To ensure consistency, the system must generate a unique Sales Order ID right away and assign incremental item numbers without waiting for activation.

Importance:

  • Early numbering guarantees unique identification across drafts and active records.

  • Associations ensure header–item linkage is preserved.

  • Draft handling allows users to save incomplete transactions safely and activate later without losing data integrity.

Behavior Definition of Root Entity

early numbering → enables key generation during root creation.

unmanaged implementation in class zbp_i_vd_head unique;
strict ( 2 );
with draft;

define behavior for ZI_VD_HEAD alias Header
draft table zdft_vd_head
lock master
total etag CreatedOn
authorization master ( instance )
early numbering // Early numbering declaration in root entity
{
  create;
  update;
  delete;
  association _item { create; }
  field ( readonly : update ) CustNo;

  draft action Edit;
  draft action Activate optimized;
  draft action Resume;
  draft action Discard;
  draft determine action Prepare;
}
mapping for zvd_t_hsale {
  CustNo     = cust_no;
  SalesOrder = sales_order;
  CreatedOn  = created_on;
  EntryTime  = entry_time;
  Name       = name;
  DocCat     = doc_cat;
}


Behavior Definition of Item Entity

early numbering → enables key generation during item creation.

define behavior for ZI_VD_ITEM alias Item
draft table zdft_vd_item
lock dependent by _head
authorization dependent by _head
early numbering // Early numbering declaration in item entity
{
  update;
  delete;
  field ( readonly ) CustNo;
  association _head;

  mapping for zvd_t_isale {
    CustNo     = cust_no;
    SalesOrder = sales_order;
    DocItem    = doc_item;
    MatNumber  = mat_number;
    MatGroup   = mat_group;
    CreatedOn  = created_on;
  }
}

Behavior Pool Implementation

This method ensures that every new entity gets a unique identifier (CustNo) during creation if it wasn’t already provided.

UUID Generation:

  • cl_system_uuid=>create_uuid_x16_static( ) generates a globally unique key.

  • This avoids collisions and guarantees uniqueness across drafts and active records.

Mapped Root:

  • %cid → correlates client-side temporary IDs with backend entities.

  • %key → holds the generated or existing key.

  • %is_draft → ensures draft state is preserved.

  • CustNo → the actual business key stored in the entity.

METHOD earlynumbering_create.
  LOOP AT entities INTO DATA(entity) WHERE CustNo IS INITIAL.
    DATA(ls_uuid) = cl_system_uuid=>create_uuid_x16_static( ).

    APPEND VALUE #( %cid      = entity-%cid
                    %key      = VALUE #( CustNo = ls_uuid )
                    %is_draft = entity-%is_draft
                    CustNo    = ls_uuid ) TO mapped-header.
  ENDLOOP.

  LOOP AT entities INTO entity WHERE CustNo IS NOT INITIAL.
    APPEND VALUE #( %cid      = entity-%cid
                    %key      = entity-%key
                    %is_draft = entity-%is_draft ) TO mapped-header.
  ENDLOOP.
ENDMETHOD.

Mapped Item:

This method handles early numbering when creating items via association (CREATE-BY-ASSOCIATION).

  • First, it retrieves the maximum cust_no from the database table zvd_t_isale for the current entity.

  • Then, it checks existing items in the helper class buffer (gt_isale) to ensure numbering continuity.

  • For each new item (DocItem IS INITIAL), it increments the maximum by 10 and assigns the new number.

  • %cid → client-side correlation ID.

  • %key → generated key with CustNo.

  • %is_draft → preserves draft state.

  • CustNo → ensures linkage to the root entity.

METHOD earlynumbering_cba_item.
  LOOP AT entities INTO DATA(entity).
    SELECT MAX( cust_no ) FROM zvd_t_isale
      INTO (lv_max)
      WHERE cust_no = -CustNo.

    LOOP AT zcl_vd_helper=>get_instance( )->gt_isale INTO DATA(item)
      WHERE cust_no = entity-%key-CustNo.
      IF item-doc_item > lv_max.
        lv_max = item-doc_item.
      ENDIF.
    ENDLOOP.

    LOOP AT entity-%target INTO DATA(t_item) WHERE DocItem IS INITIAL.
      lv_max += 10.

      APPEND VALUE #( %cid      = t_item-%cid
                      %key      = VALUE #( CustNo = entity-%key-CustNo )
                      %is_draft = t_item-%is_draft
                      CustNo    = entity-%key-CustNo ) TO mapped-item.
    ENDLOOP.
  ENDLOOP.
ENDMETHOD.

  • Item inherits lock and authorization from the header.

  • Early numbering ensures item keys are generated consistently.

  • Association links items back to the header BO.

Persistence Methods

  • Create Method
METHOD create.
  LOOP AT entities INTO DATA(entity).
    DATA(header) = CORRESPONDING zvd_t_hsale( entity MAPPING FROM ENTITY ).

    header-created_on = sy-datum.
    header-entry_time = sy-datum.
    header-name       = sy-uname.

    APPEND header TO gt_hsale.
  ENDLOOP.
ENDMETHOD.
  • Create-By-Association Method
METHOD cba.
  LOOP AT entities_cba INTO DATA(entity_cba).
    LOOP AT entity_cba-%target INTO DATA(target_item).
      DATA(item) = CORRESPONDING zvd_t_isale( target_item MAPPING FROM ENTITY ).

      item-cust_no   = entity_cba-%key-CustNo.
      item-created_on = sy-datum.

      APPEND item TO gt_isale.
    ENDLOOP.
  ENDLOOP.
ENDMETHOD.

Result

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

Conclusion

By combining Draft Handling, Associations, and Early Numbering, RAP unmanaged scenarios can support complex business objects like Sales Orders, Purchase Orders. This approach ensures flexibility, control, and consistency in transactional applications.

Thank You...

entry.title: Link Handlers cannot be registered as the application is signed - Eclipse MAC
entry.content:
What Are Link Handlers?

When you click an adt:// link somewhere — SAP GUI, a wiki, a colleague's message — macOS needs to figure out which app should open it. That mapping is handled by link handlers.

In Eclipse, you find them under Preferences → General → Link Handlers. Two entries matter for ABAP developers:

  • adt – How adt:// links open ABAP objects (classes, programs, etc.) in your Eclipse workspace.
  • eclipse+command – Lets URLs trigger Eclipse commands, like opening a specific view.

Both should show a checked box and "This application" in the Handler column. If you only have one Eclipse on your Mac, you might never notice a problem. But with two installations (say one for ABAP, one for Java), wrong link routing gets annoying fast.

The Problem

You open Link Handlers and see this at the top:

⛔Link Handlers cannot be registered as the application is signed.

Checkboxes grayed out. Handler column says "Other application." Sometimes you also get this when clicking around the page:

Cannot invoke "org.eclipse.urischeme.ISchemeInformation.schemeIsHandledByOther()"
because "schemeInfo.information" is null

You click an adt:// link and either the wrong Eclipse opens, or nothing happens.

Why This Happens on macOS

macOS maps URL schemes to apps using the Info.plist inside each app bundle (/Applications/Eclipse.app/Contents/Info.plist). A key called CFBundleURLTypes lists which schemes the app claims. macOS reads it and stores the mapping in its Launch Services database. On top of that, plugins like ADT can also register schemes at runtime.

Here's where it breaks. Eclipse ships code-signed. The signature covers the entire bundle, including Info.plist. When Eclipse tries to modify the plist at runtime to register a scheme, macOS blocks it because that would invalidate the signature. The Link Handlers page sees the restriction and grays everything out.

The Fix

Two parts to this: first, make sure your Info.plist has the right URL scheme entries, then fix the code signature that's blocking everything.

⚠️Close Eclipse before you start.

Step 1: Check your Info.plist for CFBundleURLTypes

Open the plist:

sudo nano /Applications/Eclipse.app/Contents/Info.plist

Look for a CFBundleURLTypes key. The default Eclipse plist usually already has eclipse+command and eclipse+mpc, but not adt. You need all three in a single CFBundleURLTypes array. If the key already exists, add the adt entry into the existing array. If it's missing entirely, add this block just before the closing </dict> at the end of the file:

<key>CFBundleURLTypes</key>
<array>
    <dict>
        <key>CFBundleURLName</key>
        <string>Eclipse Command</string>
        <key>CFBundleURLSchemes</key>
        <array>
            <string>eclipse+command</string>
        </array>
    </dict>
    <dict>
        <key>CFBundleURLName</key>
        <string>Eclipse Marketplace</string>
        <key>CFBundleURLSchemes</key>
        <array>
            <string>eclipse+mpc</string>
        </array>
    </dict>
    <dict>
        <key>CFBundleURLName</key>
        <string>ADT Link Handler</string>
        <key>CFBundleURLSchemes</key>
        <array>
            <string>adt</string>
        </array>
    </dict>
</array>

Save and exit (Ctrl+O, then Ctrl+X).

Important: Make sure there's only one CFBundleURLTypes key in the entire file. If you're adding the adt entry to an existing array, don't create a second CFBundleURLTypes block — duplicate keys in a plist don't merge. macOS only reads one of them, and you'll get unpredictable results. I made this mistake editing in nano and it caused the schemeInfo.information is null error.

Step 2: Remove the existing code signature

sudo codesign --remove-signature /Applications/Eclipse.app

Don't try to open Eclipse after this. macOS won't launch an unsigned app. Move straight to Step 3.

Step 3: Re-sign with an ad-hoc signature

sudo codesign --force --deep --sign - /Applications/Eclipse.app

The - means ad-hoc — no developer certificate needed. --deep signs all the nested binaries inside the bundle too.

Step 4: Re-register with Launch Services

/System/Library/Frameworks/CoreServices.framework/Versions/A/Frameworks/LaunchServices.framework/Versions/A/Support/lsregister -R -f /Applications/Eclipse.app

Tells macOS to re-read the app bundle and update its URL scheme mappings.

Step 5: Open Eclipse and check

Go to Preferences → General → Link Handlers. Both adt and eclipse+command should now show checked and marked "This application".

All terminal commands together

# Close Eclipse first, then:
sudo codesign --remove-signature /Applications/Eclipse.app
sudo codesign --force --deep --sign - /Applications/Eclipse.app
/System/Library/Frameworks/CoreServices.framework/Versions/A/Frameworks/LaunchServices.framework/Versions/A/Support/lsregister -R -f /Applications/Eclipse.app
# Open Eclipse → Preferences → Link Handlers

What I Tried That Didn't Work

Sharing these so you don't waste time on the same dead ends.

Re-signing without removing first. Tried just running codesign --force --deep --sign - on its own. Didn't help. The old signature's restrictions stuck around somehow. You have to remove first, then sign fresh.

Duplicate CFBundleURLTypes keys in the plist. This one cost me the most time. When editing in nano, I accidentally added a second CFBundleURLTypes block instead of adding entries into the existing one. Plist files don't merge duplicate keys — macOS only reads one of them. The result was the schemeInfo.information is null error, and eclipse+command stayed stuck on "Other application." If you see that error, open the plist and check for duplicate keys.

Running lsregister by itself. Doesn't do anything useful if the signature is still blocking registration. Necessary as a final step, but not enough on its own.

Environment

Tested on Eclipse 2025-03 (4.39) with ADT on macOS. Should work the same on any recent Eclipse version where you see the signed-application warning.

entry.title: Object Release State in Cloudification Repository Viewer for Clean Core Extensibility
entry.content:
Introduction

The updated Clean Core Extensibility whitepaper was introduced in August 2025 by SAP as an evolution of the 3-Tier Extensibility concept. This updated the 3-Tier Extensibility to a more detailed assessment of classic ABAP extensions based on the clean core level concept, allowing you as SAP customers and partners stepwise transformation to clean core at your own pace .

While using external APIs in your ABAP custom code, one of the first things to check as per the updated level concept is the release state of that API in the Cloudification Repository Viewer. The release state tells you how stable the object is, what contractual guarantee SAP provides for it, and how your ATC checks will treat any usage of it. Getting this right is important because it directly affects your clean core compliance and your upgrade readiness.

SAP categorizes all objects in the Cloudification Repository Viewer into five distinct states. Each state carries a specific meaning for your custom code, and knowing the difference will help you make better decisions when selecting or replacing APIs.

The Five Release States in the Cloudification Repository Viewer

Here is what each state means in practice and what you should do when you encounter it.

Released

A Released API is one that SAP has formally published with a stability contract. Backward compatibility is guaranteed across upgrades, so you can build on these objects with confidence. Released APIs correspond to clean core Level A.

Level A objects are 100% Cloud compliant, 100% Clean core, can be achieved On-Stack, Side-by-Side or Hybrid.

If you are starting new development, Released APIs are your first choice. They represent the safest and most future-proof option available to you.

Deprecated

A Deprecated API was once Released but SAP is now phasing it out. It still functions today, but it has a planned end of life. SAP recommends that you do not start any new development on it.

If your existing code already uses a Deprecated object, that code is treated as clean core Level B and flagged as Priority 3 in ATC checks. Make sure to identify the successor API in the Cloudification Repository or the SAP Business Accelerator Hub and plan your migration. The sooner you move away from a Deprecated API, the less remediation work you will face when the object reaches its end-of-life date.

Classic API

Classic APIs are well-known, time-tested objects such as BAPIs and RFC-enabled function modules that SAP product experts nominated before a formal release process existed. They do not carry a formal stability contract, but they are upgrade stable and widely used across the SAP ecosystem.

Classic APIs correspond to clean core Level B and are flagged as Priority 3 in ATC checks. Keep in mind that while these objects are stable in practice, they do not carry the same forward-compatibility guarantee as Released APIs. Use them when no Released alternative is available and check the Cloudification Repository Viewer for the availability of the Released APIs as successors to Classic APIs.

Not to be Released

An API marked as Not to be Released is one that SAP has explicitly decided is not intended for external use. Any exemption for using such an object must include a migration plan toward a Released API.

These objects correspond to clean core Level C depending on the context and are flagged as Priority 2 in ATC checks. If you encounter this state, treat it as a signal to find a supported alternative as quickly as possible.

No API

An object classified as No API has no API contract whatsoever. Objects classified as noAPI must not be used, creates maximum technical debt.

This state is flagged as Priority 1 in ATC checks and must be addressed at the highest priority. If you find a noAPI object in your custom code, replacing it with a Released API must be your immediate next step.

Why Some APIs Are Listed as Both Released and Classic or Not to be Released and Classic

You may come across a scenario in the Cloudification Repository Viewer where the same API appears under both the Classic API and Released API categories or Not to be Released and Classic. This is a valid situation and worth understanding, because it can cause some confusion at first glance.

 

Take the CDS view I_EARMARKEDFUNDSDOCUMENTITEM as an example. If you check this object in the Cloudification Repository Viewer for the 2022 SP0 release of SAP Cloud ERP Private, it is listed only as a Classic API. When you switch to the 2025 FPS01 release, it appears as both Classic API and Released API.

 

 

What this tells you is that I_EARMARKEDFUNDSDOCUMENTITEM was formally Released in the 2025 FPS0 release of SAP Cloud ERP Private.

Both statuses are equally valid for this object. It carries the historical stability of a Classic API and now also has a formal stability contract as a Released API. In this case, the clean core status will be Level A as Released API will take the precedence and ATC check will not give any message.

Similarly, take the Class CL_HTTP_UTILITY as an example. If you check this object in the Cloudification Repository Viewer for the 2022 SP0 release of SAP Cloud ERP Private, it is listed only as a Classic API. When you switch to the 2025 FPS0 release, it appears as both Classic API and Not to be Released, with a successor.

Both statuses are equally valid for this object. It carries the historical stability of a Classic API and now also has a formal stability contract as a Not to be Released, with a successor.

In this case, the clean core status will be Level B as Classic API will take the precedence and ATC check will give a Priority 3 message. You are recommended to move to the successor API in this case.

This is why it is recommended to check the Cloudification Repository Viewer against the specific release you are working with.

Conclusion

The Cloudification Repository Viewer continues to grow with each release as SAP adds more objects and updates their states. Use it as your starting point before selecting any API in your custom code. Checking the release state upfront will save you significant remediation work during upgrade cycles and help you stay clean core compliant from day one.

We will continue to share updates on clean core extensibility as the tooling and API landscape evolves. In the meantime, refer to the Cloudification Repository Viewer and the SAP Business Accelerator Hub for the latest state of the objects relevant to your development.
